weeks.title: Courts with Civil Jurisdiction
weeks.description: Jurisdiction of Nigerian courts in civil matters, constitutional provisions, election tribunals and composition of courts.

questions.scenario: Recently, in the light of the harsh economy caused by the Covid 19 Pandemic, the House of Assembly of Lagos State has, in order to maintain the lockdown directive of the President and generate income for the State, passed a law called Aircraft Prohibition Law, 2020, requiring all flights to Lagos State to pay N1, 000,000 as fine in breach of the lockdown order. The National Assembly is aggrieved feeling that the law is a clear usurpation of their power because matters of aviation are under the Exclusive Legislative List of the Constitution. The National Assembly has thus decided to sue the House of Assembly of Lagos state.
questions.question: Which court would have the jurisdiction to entertain the action?
questions.explanation: Dispute between the National Assembly and the House of Assembly of a State is within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Sec. 1 of the Supreme Court (Additional Original Jurisdiction) Act, 2002.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 1, Supreme Court (Additional Original Jurisdiction) Act, 2002

questions.scenario: Recently, in the light of the harsh economy caused by the Covid 19 Pandemic, the House of Assembly of Lagos State has, in order to maintain the lockdown directive of the President and generate income for the State, passed a law called Aircraft Prohibition Law, 2020, requiring all flights to Lagos State to pay N1, 000,000 as fine in breach of the lockdown order. The National Assembly is aggrieved feeling that the law is a clear usurpation of their power because matters of aviation are under the Exclusive Legislative List of the Constitution. The National Assembly has thus decided to sue the House of Assembly of Lagos state.
questions.question: The appropriate constitution of the Court you have identified above will be:
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court will be constituted by not less than 7 Justices in the following matters: 1. Where it is a case of its original jurisdiction, 2. Appeals on fundamental human right cases; 3. Interpretation of the Constitution and 4. When it wants to overrule itself.

questions.scenario: Recently, in the light of the harsh economy caused by the Covid 19 Pandemic, the House of Assembly of Lagos State has, in order to maintain the lockdown directive of the President and generate income for the State, passed a law called Aircraft Prohibition Law, 2020, requiring all flights to Lagos State to pay N1, 000,000 as fine in breach of the lockdown order. The National Assembly is aggrieved feeling that the law is a clear usurpation of their power because matters of aviation are under the Exclusive Legislative List of the Constitution. The National Assembly has thus decided to sue the House of Assembly of Lagos state.
questions.question: The parties in the above suit will be:
options: The National Assembly v the House of Assembly, Lagos State
The Senate President v House of Assembly, Lagos State
The National Assembly v Speaker, House of Assembly, Lagos State
The Senate President v Speaker, House of Assembly, Lagos State
questions.explanation: The nominal party where the National Assembly sues or is sued is 'National Assembly', while for the House of Assembly; it is 'Speaker of the House of Assembly'. Sec. 3, Supreme Court Additional Jurisdiction Act.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 3, Supreme Court Additional Jurisdiction Act

questions.scenario: Recently, in the light of the harsh economy caused by the Covid 19 Pandemic, the House of Assembly of Lagos State has, in order to maintain the lockdown directive of the President and generate income for the State, passed a law called Aircraft Prohibition Law, 2020, requiring all flights to Lagos State to pay N1, 000,000 as fine in breach of the lockdown order. The National Assembly is aggrieved feeling that the law is a clear usurpation of their power because matters of aviation are under the Exclusive Legislative List of the Constitution. The National Assembly has thus decided to sue the House of Assembly of Lagos state.
questions.question: Before the suit can be brought by the National Assembly, a resolution must have been passed by:
options: Two-third majority of total members of the Senate alone
Two-third majority of members of both houses present and voting
Simple majority of members of both houses present and voting
Simple majority of total members of the Senate present and voting
questions.explanation: Before a suit is commenced by the National Assembly, a resolution must have been passed by simple majority of members of both Houses present and voting. Sec. 2, Supreme Court Additional Jurisdiction Act.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 2, Supreme Court Additional Jurisdiction Act

questions.scenario: Recently, in the light of the harsh economy caused by the Covid 19 Pandemic, the House of Assembly of Lagos State has, in order to maintain the lockdown directive of the President and generate income for the State, passed a law called Aircraft Prohibition Law, 2020, requiring all flights to Lagos State to pay N1, 000,000 as fine in breach of the lockdown order. The National Assembly is aggrieved feeling that the law is a clear usurpation of their power because matters of aviation are under the Exclusive Legislative List of the Constitution. The National Assembly has thus decided to sue the House of Assembly of Lagos state.
questions.question: All except one is incorrect about the judgment of the Court you have identified in 1 above:
options: It can be appealed to the Court of Appeal
It can be appealed to the Supreme Court
It is a final decision not subject to appeal
It can only be appealed to the Supreme Court with leave
questions.explanation: The decision of the Supreme Court is final and not subject to appeal. Sec. 235, 1999 CFRN.

questions.question: One of the following statements is untrue: 'the Supreme Court constitutes a full court when it wants to...'
options: Sit on Fundamental human right cases
Sit on Governorship election tribunal as the final court
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court constitutes a full court (7 Justices) for: original jurisdiction cases, appeals on fundamental human rights, interpretation of the Constitution, and when it wants to overrule itself. Sitting on Governorship election tribunal as final court is NOT one of the circumstances.

questions.scenario: Following the recent executive regulation to lock down Abuja, Lagos and Ogun state. The Governor of Ogun state boasted to the press that the President cannot do such in Ogun state because the proper order of procedure was not followed. The National Assembly agreed to this too. The President, feeling that his power has been challenged decided to deploy the army to Ogun state to enforce his regulation. However, with recourse to the advice of his close friend, Frank SAN, he decided to sue the National Assembly and the Governor of Ogun state.
questions.question: He decided to sue the National Assembly and the Governor of Ogun state in....... court
questions.explanation: The parties do not come under the jurisdiction of the Supreme Court. Joining any party other than the ones stated in the Act will 'rob' the Supreme Court of the jurisdiction. See the case of A.G. Ondo State v A.G.F.

questions.question: The Constitutional composition of the Supreme Court is:
options: The CJN and such number of Justices as may be determined by the NJC
The CJN and such number of Justices as may be determined by the President
The CJN and such number of Justices not less than 21
The CJN and such number of Justices not exceeding 21
questions.explanation: The composition of the Supreme Court is the CJN and such number of Justices not exceeding 21. Sec. 230, CFRN.

questions.scenario: Yesufu a Supreme Court Justice was worried that by age ___, he may retire from office because of his fragile health and he has not stayed up to 2 years in office. His older friend, Asamu JCA cautioned him and said 'what about me that would be forced to cease to hold office at the age of (10) ___ so cheer up.'
questions.question: At what age may a Justice of the Supreme Court retire? (Question 9)
questions.explanation: A Justice of the Supreme Court or the Court of Appeal may retire when he attains the age of 65, but shall retire when he attains the age of 70. Sec. 291 CFRN.

questions.scenario: Yesufu a Supreme Court Justice was worried that by age ___, he may retire from office because of his fragile health and he has not stayed up to 2 years in office. His older friend, Asamu JCA cautioned him and said 'what about me that would be forced to cease to hold office at the age of (10) ___ so cheer up.'
questions.question: At what age is a Justice of the Court of Appeal forced to cease to hold office? (Question 10)
questions.explanation: A Justice of the Supreme Court or the Court of Appeal shall retire when he attains the age of 70. Sec. 291, CFRN.

questions.scenario: At the Annual General Meeting of Oluwaglass Nigeria PLC, a dispute ensued between one of the directors of the company, Mr. Agbalumo and a shareholder, Mr. Opeyinbo. In the course of the dispute, both parties started exchanging words and suddenly, Mr. Opeyinbo called Mr. Agbalumo a pig who is only good at sleeping with other men's wives. Mr. Agbalumo was furious and threatened to bring a defamation suit against Mr. Opeyinbo.
questions.question: Which court will have jurisdiction over the action?
options: State High Court/High Court of the Federal Capital Territory
questions.explanation: The subject matter is defamation, a personal action and not definitely an action arising from CAMA to confer jurisdiction on the FHC.

questions.scenario: At the Annual General Meeting of Oluwaglass Nigeria PLC, a dispute ensued between one of the directors of the company, Mr. Agbalumo and a shareholder, Mr. Opeyinbo. In the course of the dispute, both parties started exchanging words and suddenly, Mr. Opeyinbo called Mr. Agbalumo a pig who is only good at sleeping with other men's wives. Mr. Agbalumo was furious and threatened to bring a defamation suit against Mr. Opeyinbo.
questions.question: Assuming the action was instituted in the Lagos State High Court, and the court holds that it has no jurisdiction, the proper order to make is to:
questions.explanation: Generally, where a court holds that it has no jurisdiction to try a case, the proper order to make is to strike it out.

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the candidate of the Youth Must Lead Political Party, Obafemi Thanni was declared winner of the election, while the candidate of Creative Nigerian Youth, Asundemade Habeeb, was declared the first runner up. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result claiming that the President elect was invalidly elected and that he should be declared winner instead.
questions.question: Which court has jurisdiction over Asundemade Habeeb's claim?
questions.explanation: The Court of Appeal has exclusive original jurisdiction to determine if a person has been validly elected into the office of the President or Vice President. Thus, a petition to contest the result of a presidential election is to be filed at the Court of Appeal. Sec. 239(1), CFRN.

questions.scenario: The AGF, Lere Asamu was prosecuting Mr Oladokun, a drug maffia in High court of FCT for dealings in narcotics. Frank SAN, the defence counsel raised objection that the court lacks jurisdiction to entertain the matter. The Judge, Lizzy Baby J. has called on you as amicus curiae to tell her the appropriate thing to do in this circumstance.
questions.question: What would you advise?
questions.explanation: Note that the Rules of the FCT Courts gives a Judge power to transfer matters to court with competent jurisdiction. If it were to be in Lagos, the answer would have been A, to strike out the case. O. 41, R. 6, FCT Rules, 2018.

questions.question: Chairmen of Election tribunals are appointed by
options: President of Court of Appeal in consultation with Heads of Court of state
CJN in consultation with Heads of Court of state
questions.explanation: Chairmen of election tribunals are appointed by the President of the Court of Appeal in consultation with Heads of court of state.

questions.question: Appeals lie from the decision of the Court of Appeal to the Supreme Court. However, certain decisions of the Court of Appeal are final and not appealable to the Supreme Court. These decisions include one of the following:
options: Decisions from the Code of conduct tribunal
Decisions from the Legislative Houses Election Tribunal
questions.explanation: Decision of the Court of Appeal on legislative houses election petition is final. Sec. 246(3), CFRN.

questions.question: The total number of Justices of the Court of Appeal is:
questions.explanation: Sec. 1, Court of Appeal Act.

questions.scenario: In late Mr. Akerodolu Mimiko's Will dated 12th January 2020, Mr. Akeredolu gave one of his Concubines, Mrs Eyinjuoluwa Inumidun his 4 storey building in Ikoyi. His wife, Mrs Temilade Mimiko, claimed that the Will was invalidly executed. She has come to you as her lawyer to institute an action in Court contesting the validity of the Will.
questions.question: In which of the following Courts would you institute the action?
options: High Court of Lagos Ikoyi Judicial Division
questions.explanation: Customary or Magistrate Court do not have jurisdiction to try cases involving validity of a Will.

questions.scenario: Mr Chuks who had lived in the house as a tenant during the lifetime of Mr. Akeredolu, claims that the title of the land belongs to him as he bought it from Akeredolu in 2018. He has decided to institute an action in Court for declaration of title to land.
questions.question: The appropriate court to institute the action is:
questions.explanation: Disputes relating to title to land is not for the Customary Court or the Magistrate Court. Sec. 28, Magistrates Court Law, Lagos State.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 28, Magistrates Court Law, Lagos State

questions.scenario: Peter and Paul are close friends. Peter, who didn't have an account with Heritage Bank, approached the Bank for a loan of ₦50,000,000. Paul agreed to be a guarantor when the Bank requested a guarantor who is a customer with strong financial standing in the Bank. Peter has defaulted in repaying the loan and the Bank has decided to sue Paul who the Bank believes is more financially buoyant to pay.
questions.question: Which Court has jurisdiction over the case?
questions.explanation: In disputes involving banker-customer relationship, both the Federal High Court and States High Court/FCT have concurrent jurisdiction. NDIC v Okem Enterprises. Proviso to Sec. 251(1)(d), CFRN.
questions.law_reference: Proviso to Sec. 251(1)(d), CFRN; NDIC v Okem Enterprises

questions.question: All but one of the following courts is clothed with civil jurisdiction:
questions.explanation: Magistrates Court in the North are only vested with criminal jurisdiction. District Courts are vested with civil jurisdiction.

questions.question: All except one are areas where the Federal High Court has exclusive jurisdiction:
options: Matters arising from the provisions of CAMA
Dispute pertaining to banker Customers Relationship
Disputes pertaining to mining and mineral exploration.
questions.explanation: In disputes involving banker-customer relationship, both the Federal High Court and States High Court/FCT have concurrent jurisdiction. See comment on question 20. Sec. 251(1)(d), CFRN.

questions.question: By the Supreme Court (Additional Original Jurisdiction) Act 2002, the three additional exclusive original jurisdictions conferred on the Supreme Court does not include dispute between:
options: The National Assembly and the President
The Vice President and a State
The National Assembly and a State
The National Assembly and any State House of Assembly
questions.explanation: The additional jurisdictions under the Supreme Court (Additional Original Jurisdiction) Act 2002 do not include disputes between the Vice President and a State.

questions.question: Where a suit is instituted in accordance with the provisions of the Supreme Court (Additional Jurisdictions) Act, which of the following will NOT be a party?
options: The President of the Federal Republic of Nigeria
The Speaker of a State House of Assembly
The Attorneys-General of the Federation or States
questions.explanation: See sec. 20 of the Supreme Court (S.C.) Act as well.

questions.question: In a dispute between the Federation and the National Assembly, the appropriate parties are:
options: The President Federal Republic of Nigeria v National Assembly
The Attorney General of the Federation v National Assembly
Federal Executive v Speaker of the National Assembly
The President Federal Republic of Nigeria v The Senate President
questions.explanation: Disputes involving the Federation, the nominal party shall be the AGF. Sec. 20, S.C. Act.

questions.scenario: Recently the House of Assembly of Ekiti State impeached the Governor. This led to a power tussle between the Deputy Governor and the Speaker of the State House of Assembly on who will succeed the Governor. In view of the chaos, the Executive President GCFR declared a state of Emergency in the State. Thereafter, a military administrator was appointed to take charge of the state. The EFCC also declared the Governor wanted on grounds of misappropriation of State Funds. The Governor is aggrieved and wishes to seek redress. He has approached you.
questions.question: If he wants to sue the State House of Assembly for a declaration that his impeachment and removal is unlawful and unconstitutional, which court should he go to?
questions.explanation: Sec. 272(3) CFRN. That provision giving power to FHC to determine the question of whether the office of the Governor of a State has become vacant has been criticized by many authors. But the law is the law.

questions.scenario: Recently the House of Assembly of Ekiti State impeached the Governor. This led to a power tussle between the Deputy Governor and the Speaker of the State House of Assembly on who will succeed the Governor. In view of the chaos, the Executive President GCFR declared a state of Emergency in the State. Thereafter, a military administrator was appointed to take charge of the state. The EFCC also declared the Governor wanted on grounds of misappropriation of State Funds. The Governor is aggrieved and wishes to seek redress. He has approached you.
questions.question: Assuming the Attorney General of Ekiti wants to institute an action against the Federation seeking a declaration that the proclamation of the State of Emergency is inconsistent with the constitution thus null, which court would have jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Disputes between the federation and a state is within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.

questions.scenario: Recently the House of Assembly of Ekiti State impeached the Governor. This led to a power tussle between the Deputy Governor and the Speaker of the State House of Assembly on who will succeed the Governor. In view of the chaos, the Executive President GCFR declared a state of Emergency in the State. Thereafter, a military administrator was appointed to take charge of the state. The EFCC also declared the Governor wanted on grounds of misappropriation of State Funds. The Governor is aggrieved and wishes to seek redress. He has approached you.
questions.question: If the National Assembly wants to institute an action against Ekiti State House of Assembly on the ground that the panel which removed the Governor was not duly constituted, which court would have jurisdiction?
questions.explanation: Dispute between the National Assembly and a State's House of Assembly is within the exclusive jurisdiction of the Supreme Court.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 1, Supreme Court (Additional Original Jurisdiction) Act, 2002

questions.scenario: Recently the House of Assembly of Ekiti State impeached the Governor. This led to a power tussle between the Deputy Governor and the Speaker of the State House of Assembly on who will succeed the Governor. In view of the chaos, the Executive President GCFR declared a state of Emergency in the State. Thereafter, a military administrator was appointed to take charge of the state. The EFCC also declared the Governor wanted on grounds of misappropriation of State Funds. The Governor is aggrieved and wishes to seek redress. He has approached you.
questions.question: Assuming the Governor wants to challenge the validity of the election of a member of the State House of Assembly, which court should he go to?
options: There is a Governorship Election Petition Tribunal
National and State House of Assembly Election Petition Tribunal
questions.explanation: Challenges to the validity of elections into State Houses of Assembly go to the National and States Houses of Assembly Election Petition Tribunal. Sec. 285(1), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Recently the House of Assembly of Ekiti State impeached the Governor. This led to a power tussle between the Deputy Governor and the Speaker of the State House of Assembly on who will succeed the Governor. In view of the chaos, the Executive President GCFR declared a state of Emergency in the State. Thereafter, a military administrator was appointed to take charge of the state. The EFCC also declared the Governor wanted on grounds of misappropriation of State Funds. The Governor is aggrieved and wishes to seek redress. He has approached you.
questions.question: Assuming the Governor wants to institute an action against the EFCC to restrain them from arresting him, which court will have jurisdiction to entertain the action?
questions.explanation: EFCC is a federal government agency. Sec. 251(1)(r), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Mr. James Alimi recently terminated the appointment of Sisi Akowe, his driver, who had worked with him for over 10 years. He refused to pay her the arrears of salaries for over eight months. Sisi Akowe has approached you to file an action to recover the salary arrears.
questions.question: In which court will you file the action?
options: The High Court of a State
questions.explanation: It's a dispute arising out of employment matters, falling within the exclusive jurisdiction of NIC.

questions.scenario: Mr. James Alimi recently terminated the appointment of Sisi Akowe, his driver, who had worked with him for over 10 years. He refused to pay her the arrears of salaries for over eight months. Sisi Akowe has approached you to file an action to recover the salary arrears.
questions.question: The Court above will be properly constituted with:
options: A single judge or not more than three judges as the head of court may direct
A single judge or not more than two judges as the head of the Court may direct
questions.explanation: The National Industrial Court is duly constituted by a single judge or not more than three judges as the head of court may direct.

questions.scenario: Mr. James Alimi recently terminated the appointment of Sisi Akowe, his driver, who had worked with him for over 10 years. He refused to pay her the arrears of salaries for over eight months. Sisi Akowe has approached you to file an action to recover the salary arrears.
questions.question: Assuming the judgment in the above case was given against Sisi Akowe, who made an unsuccessful appeal to the Court of Appeal, which of the following is true of the judgment of the Court of Appeal?
options: It is appealable as of right to the Supreme Court
It can only be appealed to the Supreme Court with the leave of the Court of Appeal
It can only be appealed to the Supreme Court with the leave of the Supreme Court
It is final and not appealable
questions.explanation: Sec. 243(4), CFRN.

questions.question: The monetary jurisdiction of the Small Claims Court in terms of a counter-claim is the maximum amount of:
questions.explanation: The monetary jurisdiction of the Small Claims Court for counter-claims is ₦10,000,000.

questions.question: The ECOWAS Community Court is generally composed of:
questions.explanation: The ECOWAS Community Court is generally composed of 7 Independent Judges.
questions.law_reference: ECOWAS Community Court of Justice Protocol

weeks.title: Parties to a Civil Action
weeks.description: Types of parties, joinder, representative actions, class actions, third party proceedings and nominal parties.

questions.question: The position of the Attorney General of Federation as party in the case of Plateau State v AG Federation is:
questions.explanation: In disputes involving the State or the Federation, the Attorneys General are usually nominal parties, meaning they are made parties by virtue of their office.

questions.question: All but one of the following are purposes of joinder of parties
options: Avoid multiplicity or duplicity of action
Save time and avoid abuse of court
Opportunity to correct a misspelt party
To ensure all the necessary parties are made parties to the action
questions.explanation: Opportunity to correct a misspelt party is NOT a purpose of joinder of parties.

questions.question: Alteration or change of parties is necessary in all the following instances except:
questions.explanation: Alteration or change of parties is necessary in all the instances listed — bankruptcy, devolution of interest, and death. None of the above is the correct answer as all listed instances require alteration.

questions.question: Application for alteration or change of parties is brought by way of:
options: Motion on notice supported by a written address
Motion Exparte supported with an affidavit and a written address
questions.explanation: Motion must always be accompanied by a written address and may be supported by an affidavit. Affidavits are used to depose to facts; some applications are brought solely on ground of law. In such circumstance, no affidavit shall be required. See Order 43, Lagos Rules.

questions.question: If in an action against the President of Nigeria, the president is sued instead of the Attorney General of the Federation, one of the following is the implication:
questions.explanation: On the authority of AGF v Governor, Plateau State.

questions.scenario: Chevron Nigeria Ltd, an oil company, has caused serious environmental damage to residents of Igbokoda community, Ilaje local government area of Ondo state due to oil spillage which spread into the waters, poisoning the fishes in the river and taking away their means of livelihood. The members of the community have staged a protest and have vowed to sue the company. The leaders of the community, Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola have decided to institute an action on behalf of their community. You have been briefed to represent the community.
questions.question: The most appropriate type of action you would institute is:
questions.explanation: It is not a class action because the members of the community from the scenario can be ascertained and they all have a common grievance and the same interest.

questions.scenario: Chevron Nigeria Ltd, an oil company, has caused serious environmental damage to residents of Igbokoda community, Ilaje local government area of Ondo state due to oil spillage which spread into the waters, poisoning the fishes in the river and taking away their means of livelihood. The members of the community have staged a protest and have vowed to sue the company. The leaders of the community, Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola have decided to institute an action on behalf of their community. You have been briefed to represent the community.
questions.question: One of the following is not a condition precedent before you can bring the action you have identified above
options: Written authority of the members of the community
questions.explanation: Written authority of the members of the community to bring the action must be obtained AND leave of court must be sought. Both are conditions precedent — so none of the above is the answer.

questions.scenario: Chevron Nigeria Ltd, an oil company, has caused serious environmental damage to residents of Igbokoda community, Ilaje local government area of Ondo state due to oil spillage which spread into the waters, poisoning the fishes in the river and taking away their means of livelihood. The members of the community have staged a protest and have vowed to sue the company. The leaders of the community, Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola have decided to institute an action on behalf of their community. You have been briefed to represent the community.
questions.question: The appropriate parties before the Court will be:
options: Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola v Chevron Nigeria Ltd
Members of Igbokoda Community v Chevron Nigeria Ltd
Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola (suing for themselves and on behalf of members of the Igbokoda Community v Chevron Nigeria Ltd
Mr. Ikuemonisan, Mr. Odola and Igbokoda Community v Chevron Nigeria Ltd
questions.explanation: The correct format for a representative action shows the named representatives suing for themselves and on behalf of the identified community.

questions.scenario: Chevron Nigeria Ltd, an oil company, has caused serious environmental damage to residents of Igbokoda community, Ilaje local government area of Ondo state due to oil spillage which spread into the waters, poisoning the fishes in the river and taking away their means of livelihood. The members of the community have staged a protest and have vowed to sue the company. The leaders of the community, Mr. Ikuemonisan and Mr. Odola have decided to institute an action on behalf of their community. You have been briefed to represent the community.
questions.question: The mode of applying for leave to bring a representative action is:
questions.explanation: Application for leave to bring a representative action is brought by motion ex parte.

questions.scenario: Recently, there was a surge in the price of subscription for Globalcom network which brought about uproar from different parts of the country by angry customers. Apart from that, they discovered that a minute call on the network now costs ₦100. Feeling aggrieved, some certain people have decided to bring an action on behalf of the entire angry customer of Globalcom network.
questions.question: The most appropriate type of action to bring here is;
questions.explanation: All the aggrieved parties cannot be reasonably ascertained, so a class action is the most appropriate.

questions.question: In Lagos State, one of the following cannot be brought by way of class action:
options: Construction of any written instrument including a statute
Trust property: Property subject to a trust
questions.explanation: Order 15, Rule. 13, Lagos Rules.

questions.scenario: Kunle Adisa and George Olumide were appointed as executors of the estate of Chief Odulana. Both of them decided to sue Heritage Capital PLC for the debt owed to Chief Odulana before his death.
questions.question: Which of the following reflects the appropriate parties before the Court?
options: Kunle Adisa, George Olumide (suing for themselves and on behalf of the estate of Chief Odulana) v Heritage Capital PLC
Kunle Adisa and George Olumide v Heritage Capital PLC
The estate of Chief Olumide deceased v Heritage Capital PLC
Kunle Adisa, George Olumide (suing as executors to the estate of Chief Odulana, deceased) v Heritage Capital PLC (defendant)
questions.explanation: Executors sue in their capacity as executors to the estate. The correct format identifies them as 'suing as executors to the estate of Chief Odulana, deceased'.

questions.scenario: Recently, the Kingmakers in Ajibogun Land of Oyo state conspired to remove Olayiotan Abejoye from his position as one of the traditional rulers of the land, and in his place, they appointed Afopina Abobaje. Feeling aggrieved by the deposition, Olayiotan Abejoye has gone to the High Court to challenge the action of the kingmakers. Meanwhile, the kingmakers have decided that it is expedient that Afopina Abobaje be joined as a party in the case because he will be affected by the outcome of the decision of the court.
questions.question: Afopina Abobaje can be classified as what type of party?
questions.explanation: He is a desirable party. Although his non-inclusion as a party will not affect the determination of the case, but if eventually Olayiotan Abejoye is successful, he would be affected by the decision of the court. So, his presence is desirable, not necessary.
questions.law_reference: Types of parties — desirable vs necessary

questions.scenario: Recently, the Kingmakers in Ajibogun Land of Oyo state conspired to remove Olayiotan Abejoye from his position as one of the traditional rulers of the land, and in his place, they appointed Afopina Abobaje. Feeling aggrieved by the deposition, Olayiotan Abejoye has gone to the High Court to challenge the action of the kingmakers. Meanwhile, the kingmakers have decided that it is expedient that Afopina Abobaje be joined as a party in the case because he will be affected by the outcome of the decision of the court.
questions.question: What mode will be used to bring the application for joinder before the court?
questions.explanation: Application for joinder of party is brought by motion on notice, supported with an affidavit and a written address.

questions.scenario: Recently, the Kingmakers in Ajibogun Land of Oyo state conspired to remove Olayiotan Abejoye from his position as one of the traditional rulers of the land, and in his place, they appointed Afopina Abobaje. Feeling aggrieved by the deposition, Olayiotan Abejoye has gone to the High Court to challenge the action of the kingmakers. Meanwhile, the kingmakers have decided that it is expedient that Afopina Abobaje be joined as a party in the case because he will be affected by the outcome of the decision of the court.
questions.question: If it was Afopina Abobaje that brought the application to be joined as a party in the case, then he will be properly termed:
questions.explanation: An intervener is a person who was not originally made a party to a suit, but brings an application to be joined as a party in the suit.

questions.question: Where there is a failure to join a party who ought to be joined in a proceeding before the Court, the effect is that:
options: A procedural irregularity which won't lead to striking out the case
The case will be struck out
The case will be declared non-suit
questions.explanation: Order 15, Rule 16(1), Lagos Rules.

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her technology company. The loan agreement was entered in Lagos and guaranteed by Opeyemi's boyfriend, Teetah Excellent who resides in Abuja. Opeyemi Okeke has defaulted in repaying the loan despite repeated demands from Moyo Akinsete. Moyo Akinsete has therefore decided to commence an action at the Lagos High Court against Opeyemi Okeke in order to recover the money from her.

questions.question: What's the proper mode in which the application you have identified above will be brought?
questions.explanation: One key difference between joinder of party and third party proceeding is that while application for the former is by motion on notice, application for the latter is by motion exparte.

questions.explanation: Third party proceeding is available to a defendant in an action, or a claimant, who is a defendant to a counter-claim. So it is always an option in favour of the defendant to relieve him from bearing the cost of liability alone.

questions.scenario: During your externship programme at the law firm of Bright Legal, you witnessed a briefing by Mr. Dogo Yunus. He stated that on the 10th of April, 2019 one Roland Giddy drove at top speed along the Eko Hotel Road, Victoria Island, when he ran into his Prado Jeep occupied by his wife, Mrs. Raliat Yunus and his seven year old son, Taju Yunus. The occupants of the Prado Jeep were badly injured and the Jeep was damaged beyond repairs. Mr. Yunus, his wife and his son now want to institute an action in court for negligence and to claim damages for all the loss suffered.
questions.question: The proper parties in the action are:
options: The Dogo Yunus family (Claimants) v Roland Giddy (Defendant)
Mr. Dogo Yunus, Mrs Raliat Yunus, Master Taju Yunus (Claimants) v Roland Giddy (Defendant)
Mr. Dogo Yunus, Mrs Raliat Yunus, Master Taju Yunus (an infant, suing by his guardian, Mr. Dogo Yunus) (Claimants) v Roland Giddy (Defendants)
Mr. Dogo Yunus, Mrs. Raliat Yunus, suing for themselves and their son Master Taju Yunus (claimants) v Roland Giddy (Defendant)
questions.explanation: A minor (infant) must sue through a guardian. Master Taju Yunus, being 7 years old, must be described as 'an infant, suing by his guardian, Mr. Dogo Yunus'.

Miss Harris (plaintiff) v Mrs Abike George (Defendant)
Mr. Dibia suing as an attorney (Plaintiff) v Mrs Abike George (Defendant)
questions.explanation: The most appropriate is (A) because it shows the name of the Donor and that the Donee is acting as an attorney.

weeks.title: Preliminary Considerations and Commencement of Action in the Magistrate Court

questions.topic: Preliminary Considerations and Commencement of Action in the Magistrate Court
questions.question: The effect of failure to file a pre-action notice to a statutory body where it is provided in the statute is all but one of the following
options: It prevents the court from having jurisdiction on the case
It will lead to the matter being struck out
It is a mere irregularity which can be waived
questions.explanation: Failure to file pre-action notice when statutorily required is tantamount to non-fulfillment of condition precedent, one of the elements of jurisdiction. In such instance, the Court will not have jurisdiction. It is NOT a mere irregularity.

questions.topic: Preliminary Considerations and Commencement of Action in the Magistrate Court
questions.scenario: Inspector Kadir Bashir bought 200 bags of cement from TopYega cement Ltd and made an initial payment of 50% of the total purchase price. He agreed to pay the balance within a 6 months period. The 6 months period has passed and he has defaulted in meeting his obligation. TopYega cement Ltd then decided to go to court to recover the balance. Inspector Kadir raised an objection on the point of law that the action is statute barred as it was not instituted within the three months period provided under the Public Officers Protection Act.
questions.question: As the claimant's counsel, your best response to the objection would be:
options: Right, and thus concede to it
Wrong because the Act only applies to criminal actions, not civil actions
Wrong, because the Act only protects public officers when acting in their official capacity
Wrong because the Act does not apply to officers in his category
questions.explanation: The Act applies to civil and not criminal actions, and only protects officers when acting within their official capacity. The contract in question was entered into by Inspector Kadir in his personal capacity.

weeks.title: Commencement of Action in the High Courts

questions.topic: Commencement of Action in the High Courts
questions.scenario: Babatunde Alao is the owner of a plot of land situate at No. 10, White chapel Street, Ikeja, Lagos State. Omoniyi Omisore is encroaching on the land, claiming title to the land by virtue of a certificate of occupancy. Babatunde Alao has briefed you to take up prosecution of the case against Omoniyi Omisore for declaration of title to the land.
questions.question: The most appropriate court where you would institute the action is:
options: Ikorodu Judicial Division of Lagos State High Court
Ikeja Judicial Division of Lagos State High Court
Lekki Judicial Division of Lagos State High Court
Eko Judicial Division of Lagos State High Court
questions.explanation: Order 4 Rule 1, Lagos provides that actions relating to declaration of title to land shall be commenced in the judicial division where the land is situate.

questions.topic: Commencement of Action in the High Courts
questions.scenario: Babatunde Alao is the owner of a plot of land situate at No. 10, White chapel Street, Ikeja, Lagos State. Omoniyi Omisore is encroaching on the land, claiming title to the land by virtue of a certificate of occupancy. Babatunde Alao has briefed you to take up prosecution of the case against Omoniyi Omisore for declaration of title to the land.
questions.question: What mode will you use to commence the action?
questions.explanation: Actions relating to declaration of title to land are contentious, thus must be commenced by writ. See the case of Doherty v Doherty.

Statement on oath of the witnesses.
questions.explanation: When actions are commenced by writ of summons, the facts of the case are stated in the statement of claim, not in an affidavit. Order 5, Rule 1(2), Lagos.

questions.explanation: Since it is in Lagos, failure to accompany an originating process with the necessary documents shall nullify the action. Order 5, Rule 1(3), Lagos.

questions.topic: Commencement of Action in the High Courts

Omoniyi Omisore is encroaching on the land, claiming title to the land by virtue of a certificate of occupancy. questions.question: Assuming Omoniyi Omisore lives in Abuja, to effect the service of the originating process on him in Abuja, the claimant is required to comply with which of the following?
options: The originating process must be endorsed by the Registrar that it is to be served out of Lagos State and in the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja.
The defendant is to be given a period of not less than 30 days from the date of service within which to enter an appearance.
questions.explanation: This is because in Lagos, when an originating process (OP) is to be served outside jurisdiction, it is the Legal Practitioner that endorses it. Order 9, Rule 16. And Sec. 99 of the Sheriffs and Civil Process Act (SCPA), provides that the defendant in such instance should be given not less than 30 days within which to appear or if a longer period is prescribed by the rules of Court, thus in Lagos, 42 days. So neither A nor B alone is complete/correct.
questions.law_reference: Order 9, Rule 16, Lagos Rules; Sec. 99, SCPA

weeks.title: Interlocutory Applications
weeks.description: Motions, injunctions, Mareva injunction, Anton Piller order, interpleader proceedings and affidavits.

questions.question: Interlocutory applications are applications made:
questions.explanation: An example of an interlocutory application made before the trial of an action is application for substituted service. An example of the one made after trial is stay of execution.

questions.question: All but one of the following are true of an interlocutory application:
options: It must be tied to a substantive suit
It can be used to seek for a perpetual order
It can be used to remedy some defects in the main action
questions.explanation: It is untrue that interlocutory applications can be used to seek perpetual order. Okafor v AG, Anambra State.

questions.question: Where no rules/laws are cited in a motion, the Court can still grant the motion if:
options: It was filed within a reasonable time
There is a rule/law that empowers the court to grant it
The other lawyer has no opposition
questions.explanation: Falobi v Falobi.

questions.question: The following are not required to be on notice except:
questions.explanation: To vacate an interim order, the application must be on notice.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a common feature in affidavits?
options: Name and occupation of the deponent
questions.explanation: The signatures are that of the Deponent and Commissioner for Oaths. Even if it is the claimant that deposes to the affidavit, he will sign as a deponent, not as the claimant.

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos only to find people in his premises claiming his house is theirs. He has instituted an action to remove unwanted persons from his property and also for a declaration that the property is his. As the case proceeds, he realised that Mr. James, the defendant in the suit has imminent plans to demolish the building.
questions.question: As counsel to Mr. John, what applications would you bring to prevent Mr. James from demolishing the house imminently and till the case is determined:
options: Motion on notice for interim injunction only
Motion Exparte for interim injunction only
Motion Exparte for interim injunction and motion on notice for interlocutory injunction
Motion on notice for interlocutory injunction only
questions.explanation: From the question, there are two 'evils': imminently and pending the determination of the case. Interim injunction will cater for the former, while interlocutory injunction will cater for the latter.

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos only to find people in his premises claiming his house is theirs. He has instituted an action to remove unwanted persons from his property and also for a declaration that the property is his. As the case proceeds, he realised that Mr. James, the defendant in the suit has imminent plans to demolish the building.
questions.question: Assuming you intend to file a motion on notice before the Court to prevent the demolition of the building pending the determination of the case, the appropriate parties will be:
questions.explanation: The application in the scenario is tied to a substantive suit. It is then necessary for the parties to be properly designated so that the Court can know who the claimant, defendants, applicants and respondents are.

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos only to find people in his premises claiming his house is theirs. He has instituted an action to remove unwanted persons from his property and also for a declaration that the property is his.
questions.question: If it were to be a motion Exparte that you filed, then the appropriate parties before the court will be:
questions.explanation: The word 'Respondent' will not appear for Mr. James, because motion exparte is at the instance of one party, without informing the other party. 7-Up Bottling Co. v Abiola & Sons Ltd.
questions.law_reference: 7-Up Bottling Co. v Abiola & Sons Ltd

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos only to find people in his premises claiming his house is theirs. He has instituted an action to remove unwanted persons from his property and also for a declaration that the property is his. The relationship between Mr. John and Mr. James is contractual and Mr. John has reasons to believe Mr. James wants to remove his properties from the jurisdiction of the Court to render the judgment of the court in the case nugatory.
questions.question: The application you would bring to court on behalf of Mr. John to prevent this is:
options: Motion on notice for mareva injunction
Motion on notice for Antor piller injunction
Motion on exparte for Antor piller injunction
questions.explanation: Mareva injunction is brought to restrain a defendant from removing his assets from the jurisdiction of the Court until the case is determined. It is brought by way of motion exparte.

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos. The suit against Mr. James is that of piracy of his book, titled 'Outliers, the secret of success' and Mr. John wants to seize the pirated copies and prevent further productions.
questions.question: What application would you bring to prevent this?
options: Motion on notice for mareva injunction
Motion on notice for Antor piller injunction
Motion on exparte for Antor piller injunction
questions.explanation: Antor piller injunction is by motion exparte.

questions.scenario: Mr. John recently returned from India to Lagos. The suit against Mr. James is that of piracy of his book. Mr. John wants to seize the pirated copies and prevent further productions by Anton Piller order.
questions.question: The appropriate court where the application you have identified above should be brought is
options: The High Court of Lagos State
The High Court of the Federal Capital Territory
questions.explanation: Sec. 251(1)(f), CFRN.

questions.question: The conditions for the grant of an injunction include all but one of the following:
questions.explanation: There is no requirement for undertaking as to the success of the claim. What is required is undertaking for damages.

questions.question: The statutory period between the service of a motion and hearing of same is:
options: After two months of service on the respondent
After two weeks of service on the respondent
After two days of service on the respondent
questions.explanation: Order 43, Rule 4, Lagos.

questions.question: A motion Exparte generally terminates after
questions.explanation: A motion ex parte generally terminates after 7 days.

questions.question: Where no counter affidavit is filed by the Respondent in an application for interlocutory injunction in the High Court, the Respondent will only be allowed to argue issues of:
questions.explanation: Where no counter affidavit is filed, the respondent can only argue on law alone.

questions.scenario: Yemi Ayeku sued Bukola Aboluwarin for unliquidated money he claims as damages as a result of breach of contract by Bukola. Bukola was served with the originating processes but did not file memorandum of appearance or statement of defence within the stipulated time. The court adjourned the case to a specific date and ordered that hearing notices be served on the defendant. On the adjourned date, Bukola Aboluwarin did not show up. The court further adjourned till Feb. 29. On that date, Bukola Aboluwarin showed up but brought an application to appear out of time and file statement of defence, while Yemi Ayeku has an application for default judgment.
questions.question: The court is confused as to the order in which to hear both applications and has called you as amicus curiae. Advise the Court on what it should do:
options: There is no order, so the Court can hear any first
Hear Yemi Ayeku's application only because Bukola's application has been overtaken by event
questions.explanation: Where there are two conflicting applications before the Court: one seeks to regularize or save the proceeding, while the other is 'destructive', the court would give priority to the one which is to regularize, while the other will not be heard again as it will be deemed overtaken by event. See Nalsa Team Associates v NNPC.

questions.question: In all but one of the following, a Respondent is not required to file a counter affidavit:
options: Where facts deposed to in an affidavit are untrue, but logical
Where facts deposed to in an affidavit are manifestly self contradictory
Where the Respondent wishes to oppose on point of law alone
Where the Respondent is not opposing the application
questions.explanation: Where the respondent knows that the facts deposed to in an affidavit are untrue, he must file a counter-affidavit to present the correct facts.

questions.scenario: Commercial Trust Bank Plc has been notified that one of its biggest customers Chief Akin Tanko has died. The Bank got the information via two letters from two different lawyers representing the eldest son of each of Chief Tanko's wives. None of the letters cited a letter of administration or will but each demanded that the Bank pay the credit balance in the deceased's account to their client. The Bank has consulted you as an external solicitor for advice.
questions.question: What will you advise the Bank to do?
options: Share the money in equal half and deposit in the account of the rival claimants.
Apply to the court to order the claimants to take action between themselves to determine who is entitled to the money in the account.
Pay the money to the government account
Deposit the money with the Central Bank of Nigeria.
questions.explanation: The bank should apply for interpleader proceedings — applying to the court to order the claimants to take action between themselves to determine who is entitled to the money.

questions.scenario: Commercial Trust Bank Plc has been notified that one of its biggest customers Chief Akin Tanko has died. That the bank is willing to pay the money to any person that the court directs
That the bank has a special interest in the outcome of the case
That the bank has no interest in the money other than charges for its services.
questions.explanation: Order 47, Rule 2(2), Lagos.

questions.scenario: Commercial Trust Bank Plc has been notified that one of its biggest customers Chief Akin Tanko has died. questions.question: The procedure the bank will adopt is called –
questions.explanation: The bank, holding money claimed by rival claimants, adopts the stakeholder interpleader procedure.

questions.scenario: The two eldest children of Chief Akin Tanko have filed processes in court each claiming entitlement to the credit balance in the deceased's account with Commercial Trust Bank Plc.
questions.question: What order can the court make to prevent the bank from paying the money to any of the claimants until the case is determined?
questions.explanation: Interlocutory injunction lasts till the determination of the suit.

questions.question: How is an interpleader application commenced in Abuja?
questions.explanation: By the rules of Court. Order 48, Abuja.

questions.question: When the court tells Counsel to 'move in terms', Counsel is expected to _____
options: Argue his motion fully in terms of his submission.
Simply tell the Court: 'I move in terms of my motion paper' without more.
State the rules under which the motion is brought, the prayers on the motion paper, the affidavit and adopt the written address if any.
Conduct his case in terms of the reliefs sought.
questions.explanation: When told to 'move in terms', counsel states the rules under which the motion is brought, the prayers on the motion paper, the affidavit and adopts the written address if any.

questions.question: There are two types of interpleader summons namely:
questions.explanation: The two types of interpleader proceedings are stakeholder interpleader and sheriff interpleader.

questions.question: Which of the following is incorrect about affidavit?
options: It constitutes evidence before the Court
It must contain the Oath Act clause
It must be adopted by the deponent before the Court can act on it
questions.explanation: Affidavit constitutes evidence before the court on its own; it is not conditioned on its adoption by the deponent, unlike a witness statement on oath, which must be adopted by the witness before the court can consider it as evidence.
questions.law_reference: Affidavit vs witness statement on oath

weeks.title: Default and Summary Judgment Procedure
weeks.description: Summary judgment, default judgment, undefended list procedure in Abuja, setting aside judgments.

questions.question: The main difference between summary judgment and default judgment is that summary judgment:
options: Is based on lack of defence to a claim
Is a judgment on the merit
Can easily be set aside by the court that granted it
questions.explanation: Summary judgment is based on lack of defence to the claim and it is a judgment on the merit, which cannot easily be set aside by the judge that granted it.

questions.question: The types of default judgment we have does not include
options: Judgment in default of a good defence
questions.explanation: The two types of default judgments are judgment in default of pleadings and judgment in default of appearance. 'Judgment in default of a good defence' is not a recognized type.

questions.scenario: Babatunde Oyemo sued your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of ₦400,000,000 representing his share of the profit from a joint venture allegedly entered by your client and Mr. Babatunde. Unfortunately, your client was in New York when substituted service was effected on him. He came back to Nigeria two months later to discover that he had been sued. Two days after, he was served with an application for default judgment which is slated for hearing on the 20th of May, 2020. He immediately briefed you to represent him.
questions.question: The step you will take to ensure that the case is heard on its merit and not in default is:
options: Apply for the application for default judgment to be set aside
Seek leave to file statement of defence out of time
Seek leave of court to appear out of time
Seek leave to file memorandum of appearance, statement of defence and necessary documents out of time
questions.explanation: The correct step is to seek leave to file memorandum of appearance, statement of defence and all necessary documents out of time.
questions.law_reference: Leave to appear and defend out of time

questions.scenario: Babatunde Oyemo sued your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of ₦400,000,000 representing his share of the profit from a joint venture allegedly entered by your client and Mr. Babatunde. Unfortunately, your client was in New York when substituted service was effected on him. He came back to Nigeria two months later to discover that he had been sued. Two days after, he was served with an application for default judgment which is slated for hearing on the 20th of May, 2020. He immediately briefed you to represent him.
questions.question: The mode by which the application you seek above will be brought is:
questions.explanation: It is necessary that the other party be put on notice when seeking leave to enter appearance out of time.
questions.law_reference: Application to appear out of time — mode

questions.scenario: Babatunde Oyemo sued your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of ₦400,000,000 representing his share of the profit from a joint venture allegedly entered by your client and Mr. Babatunde. Judgment had been given against your client and you would like to set it aside.
questions.question: The application you would bring would be by:
questions.explanation: Application to set aside a default judgment is by motion on notice.

questions.scenario: Babatunde Oyemo sued your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of ₦400,000,000 representing his share of the profit from a joint venture allegedly entered by your client and Mr. Babatunde. Judgment had been given against your client.
questions.question: The following are conditions to be fulfilled before a default judgment can be set aside by the granting judge except:
options: Application to set aside must be brought within a reasonable time
A good defence to the claim
questions.explanation: Order 10, Rule 11, Abuja. Evidence of payment of penalty is not a condition for setting aside a default judgment.

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her mechanised farming business. The loan agreement was entered in Lagos and guaranteed by Opeyemi's boyfriend, Teetah Excellent who resides in Abuja. Opeyemi Okeke has defaulted in repaying the loan despite repeated demands from Moyo Akinsete. Moyo Akinsete has therefore decided to commence an action at the Lagos High Court against both Opeyemi and Teetah in order to recover the money from them.
questions.question: Assuming she desires to adopt the fastest procedure to get judgment expeditiously against the defendants, which of the following procedure will be the most appropriate?
options: Commence the action under the undefended list procedure
Summary Judgment under Order 13 of the High Court of Lagos State Rules
Summary Judgment under Order 11 of the High Court of Lagos State Rules
Summary Judgment under Order 35 of the High Court of Lagos State Rules
questions.explanation: There is no undefended list procedure in the Lagos Rules and summary judgment procedure under the Rules is contained in Order 13.

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her mechanised farming business. The loan agreement was entered in Lagos and guaranteed by Opeyemi's boyfriend, Teetah Excellent who resides in Abuja. Opeyemi Okeke has defaulted in repaying the loan despite repeated demands from Moyo Akinsete. Moyo Akinsete has therefore decided to commence an action at the Lagos High Court against both Opeyemi and Teetah in order to recover the money from them. She desires to adopt the fastest procedure to get judgment expeditiously.
questions.question: In achieving the procedure you have identified above, one of the following is not part of the documents to be filed?
options: Motion on notice for summary judgment
Copies of documents to be relied upon
questions.explanation: Certificate of pre-action counseling is only peculiar to Abuja.
questions.law_reference: Order 13, Lagos Rules — summary judgment documents

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her mechanised farming business. Moyo Akinsete has therefore decided to commence an action at the Lagos High Court against both Opeyemi and Teetah in order to recover the money from them. She desires to adopt the fastest procedure to get judgment expeditiously.
questions.question: If the defendant intends to defend the suit, he is expected to file his statement of defence and other accompanying documents within how many days?
questions.explanation: Order 17, Rule 1(2), Lagos Rules. The defendant has 42 days to file statement of defence in Lagos.

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her mechanised farming business. Moyo Akinsete has therefore decided to commence an action at the Lagos High Court against both Opeyemi and Teetah in order to recover the money from them. She desires to adopt the fastest procedure to get judgment expeditiously.
questions.question: If the matter were to be before the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, your answer in 9 above would be:
questions.explanation: Order 15, Rule 1(2) Abuja. In Abuja, the defendant has 21 days to file statement of defence.

questions.scenario: At the Law Firm of Oladipo Olasope where you did your externship, your principal informed you that one of his clients, Moyo Akinsete, a very popular philanthropist who lives in Lagos Nigeria lent her childhood friend, Opeyemi Okeke the sum of ₦12,000,000 to set up her mechanised farming business. Moyo Akinsete commenced an action at the Lagos High Court against both Opeyemi and Teetah. On the date fixed for trial, the defendants did not show up, neither did they file statement of defence.
questions.question: Which of the following accurately captures the kind of judgment the claimant can obtain in that regard?
questions.explanation: For summary judgment procedure under Order 13 and 11 of Lagos and Abuja Rules respectively, if the defendant fails to file memorandum of appearance or statement of defence, the type of judgment the claimant can obtain is default judgment and not summary judgment. One key difference between the two is that the former is not on the merit, thus can easily be set aside by the court that granted it.
questions.law_reference: Order 13, Lagos Rules; Order 11, Abuja Rules

questions.question: The following, except one, are incorrect about the undefended list procedure:
options: It can be used for both liquidated and unliquidated claims
It is applicable in both Lagos and Abuja
It can be used in actions for declaration of title to land
It can only be used for liquidated monetary claim
questions.explanation: The other options are incorrect about the undefended list procedure. It's only applicable in Abuja and is limited to liquidated monetary claims. Order 35, Abuja.

questions.question: The undefended list procedure is commenced by filing:
options: Writ of Summons, Exparte motion and Affidavit
Writ of Summons, Motion on Notice and Supporting Affidavit
questions.explanation: Undefended list procedure is by filing writ of summons and affidavit stating the ground on which the claim is based and the deponent's belief that there is no defence to the claim. Order 35, Rule 1, Abuja.

questions.question: In Lagos, Order 13 procedure applies to:
options: All kinds of claim before the Lagos State High Court
Only claims for liquidated money demand
Only claims for unliquidated money demand
Only claims brought by undefended list
questions.explanation: One of the main differences between the undefended list procedure and summary judgment under Order 13/11 is that the latter is not limited to claims for liquidated monetary damages, but applies to all kinds of claims.

questions.scenario: Bobby Dude, a car dealer who resides in Obanikoro, Lagos supplied a 2019 Toyota camry model to Chief Odogwu of Oaklane Avenue, Maitama, Abuja at the cost of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira Only). On delivery of the vehicle to Chief Odogwu in his residence in Abuja, Bobby Dude was paid N5 million (Five Million Naira Only, meanwhile, the agreement between the parties states that the total cost be paid on delivery. Bobby Dude has made several efforts to get the balance of N5 million but Chief Odogwu has continued to make unfulfilled promises. You have been approached by Bobby Dude to institute action for the recovery of the debt.
questions.question: Where will you file the action to recover the debt?
options: Any High Court of competent jurisdiction
questions.explanation: This is because the contract was performed in Abuja and that is where the defendant resides. Order 3, Rule 3, Abuja.

questions.scenario: Bobby Dude, a car dealer who resides in Obanikoro, Lagos supplied a 2019 Toyota camry model to Chief Odogwu of Oaklane Avenue, Maitama, Abuja at the cost of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira Only). On delivery of the vehicle to Chief Odogwu in his residence in Abuja, Bobby Dude was paid N5 million (Five Million Naira Only). Bobby Dude has made several efforts to get the balance of N5 million but Chief Odogwu has continued to make unfulfilled promises. The action has been filed in the High Court of the FCT.
questions.question: The procedure you would adopt in order to recover the money is:
questions.explanation: In Abuja, the undefended list procedure under Order 35 is used for liquidated monetary claims.

questions.scenario: Bobby Dude, a car dealer who resides in Obanikoro, Lagos supplied a 2019 Toyota camry model to Chief Odogwu of Oaklane Avenue, Maitama, Abuja at the cost of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira Only). On delivery of the vehicle to Chief Odogwu in his residence in Abuja, Bobby Dude was paid N5 million (Five Million Naira Only). Bobby Dude has made several efforts to get the balance of N5 million but Chief Odogwu has continued to make unfulfilled promises. The action has been filed in the High Court of the FCT under undefended list procedure.
questions.question: Assuming Chief Odogwu intends to defend the suit, what should he do?
options: File a Notice of intention to defend
questions.explanation: Where a defendant intends to defend a suit under the undefended list procedure, he is expected to file a notice of intention to defend before 5 days to the return date on the writ.
questions.law_reference: Order 35, Abuja Rules — notice of intention to defend

questions.scenario: Bobby Dude, a car dealer who resides in Obanikoro, Lagos supplied a 2019 Toyota camry model to Chief Odogwu of Oaklane Avenue, Maitama, Abuja at the cost of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira Only). Chief Odogwu has not paid the balance of N5 million. The action has been filed in the High Court of the FCT under undefended list procedure.
questions.question: The time limit within which Chief Odogwu is expected to file the document you have identified above is:
options: Before 5 days to the return date on the writ
Before 7 days to the return date on the writ
Within 21 days of being served the originating process
Within 42 days of being served the originating process
questions.explanation: The notice of intention to defend must be filed before 5 days to the return date on the writ.
questions.law_reference: Order 35, Abuja Rules — time for notice to defend

questions.scenario: Bobby Dude, a car dealer who resides in Obanikoro, Lagos supplied a 2019 Toyota camry model to Chief Odogwu of Oaklane Avenue, Maitama, Abuja at the cost of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira Only). On the return date, the defendant fails to appear or files any document showing his intention to defend the suit.
questions.question: The court proceeds to judgment. The type of judgment given will be:
questions.explanation: Failure of the defendant to appear or defend under the undefended list warrants a judgment on the merit, not a default judgment. Ben Thomas Hotel Ltd v Sebi Furnitures Ltd.
questions.law_reference: Ben Thomas Hotel Ltd v Sebi Furnitures Ltd

questions.scenario: Chief Mazi Obiekwe sued Mr Zakky Toby before the High court of Lagos State, Igbosere judicial division for declaration of title to a plot of land situate at Ojota market Igbosere District and for damages for trespass. It is 2 months now after service of the processes on the defendant and he has failed to respond. As a result of this, the Claimant filed a motion on notice for default judgment. When the defendant got notice of the claimant's application, he filed a motion on notice for extension of time within which to enter appearance.
questions.question: One of the following statements is true about the motion for default judgment filed by the Claimant above:
options: It was not proper as default judgment cannot be made in cases of declaration of title to land
It should been brought by Motion ex parte
It is proper provided it was supported with an affidavit and a written address
questions.explanation: Default judgment is not available in claims for declaration of title to land — the claimant must prove the case on the merit.
questions.law_reference: Declaration of title — no default judgment

questions.question: When a suit on the undefended list comes to the court for the first time, it is for:
questions.explanation: When a suit on the undefended list comes to the court for the first time, it is for hearing.

questions.question: Where leave is given to defend under the undefended list procedure, the matter is placed on
questions.explanation: Where leave is given to a defendant to defend under the undefended list procedure, the matter is transferred to the ordinary cause list.
questions.law_reference: Order 35, Abuja Rules — transfer after leave to defend

weeks.title: Pleadings
weeks.description: Rules of pleadings, amendment, defence and reply, statement of claim, counterclaim and set off.

questions.scenario: Rasheed Ijaodola commenced an action against Yusuf Ali at the High Court of Lagos State for a declaration of title to land and claim of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira) as unliquidated damages caused by the trespass committed on the land which he claims has caused a depreciation on the value of the land. He believed that the defendant does not have any defence to the claim because he is armed with all the evidence. The originating process was served on the defendant on the 1st of March, 2020 by Barrister Sergio.
questions.question: Within how many days after receipt of the originating process is the defendant expected to file his statement of defence?
questions.explanation: A defendant in Lagos is expected to file statement of defence within 42 days of receiving an originating process. Order 17, Rule 1(2), Lagos.

questions.scenario: Rasheed Ijaodola commenced an action against Yusuf Ali at the High Court of Lagos State for a declaration of title to land and claim of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira) as unliquidated damages caused by the trespass committed on the land which he claims has caused a depreciation on the value of the land. He believed that the defendant does not have any defence to the claim because he is armed with all the evidence. The originating process was served on the defendant on the 1st of March, 2020 by Barrister Sergio.
questions.question: If the claimant decides to reply, within how many days after the receipt of the statement of defence must he file the reply?
questions.explanation: Order 17, Rule 1(4), Lagos.

questions.scenario: Rasheed Ijaodola commenced an action against Yusuf Ali at the High Court of Lagos State for a declaration of title to land and claim of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira) as unliquidated damages. The originating process was served on the defendant on the 1st of March, 2020 by Barrister Sergio.
questions.question: Assuming the defendant fails to file his statement of defence within the prescribed time period by the Rules of Court, pleadings shall be deemed closed....
questions.explanation: Order 17, Rule 18, Lagos.

questions.scenario: Rasheed Ijaodola commenced an action against Yusuf Ali at the High Court of Lagos State for the recovery of debt from the defendant in the sum of ₦100,000; in addition to the statement of defence filed by the defendant, he counterclaims that the claimant owes him the amount of ₦70,000.
questions.question: As the counsel to the claimant, which of the following is the appropriate heading of the document to be filed?
options: Reply to counter claim and defence to statement of defence
Response to statement of defence and reply to counter claim
Reply to statement of defence and defence to counter claim
Response to counter claim and reply to statement of defence
questions.explanation: The claimant is expected to reply to statement of defence and defend the counter-claim.

questions.scenario: In the originating process filed by Barrister Sergio on behalf of his client Rasheed Ijaodola, the claim of ₦10,000,000 as damages was endorsed on it, however, he omitted this claim in the statement of claim. At the trial, what is the effect of the omission?
questions.question: What is the effect of the omission of the ₦10,000,000 claim from the statement of claim?
options: It is still deemed to be properly claimed as it is contained in the originating process
It will lead to striking out of the action
The effect is based on the discretion of the court
questions.explanation: The principle is that any claim on the writ which is omitted in the statement of claim is deemed abandoned. Elf Nigeria Ltd v Sillo. But any relief which is not in the writ but claimed in statement of defence is deemed to have been properly claimed. The Statement of claim supersedes in the event of conflict between it and the Writ.

questions.scenario: Rasheed Ijaodola commenced an action against Yusuf Ali at the High Court of Lagos State for a declaration of title to land and claim of ₦10,000,000 (Ten Million Naira) as unliquidated damages caused by the trespass committed on the land.
questions.question: All but one of the following are essential parts of the statement of claim filed by Rasheed Ijaodola
questions.explanation: The parts of a statement of defence are: introductory part also called matters of inducement, main body and the reliefs sought. All listed are essential parts.

questions.question: All of the following are incorrect about set off except:
options: It can be used as a shield as well as a sword
It can only be used where there are monetary claims
It is used specifically for monetary claims and declaratory reliefs
It is available for both a claimant and a defendant in a suit
questions.explanation: Set off is only applicable for monetary claims. It is a shield and not a sword, available only to the defendant in an action.

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry with number plate 30,409,ABJ driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. Dr. Lukeman was unconscious and was rushed to the hospital where he received treatment worth of ₦10 million. He has decided to sue Mr. Holloway and Better Sand Nigeria Ltd at the High Court of the FCT, Gwagalada Judicial Division for negligence. He has instructed you to pursue the claim on his behalf.
questions.question: When will the statement of claim be filed in this case?
options: 21 days after filing the writ of summons
It will be filed along with the writ of summons
14 days after filing the writ of summons
questions.explanation: A statement of claim is one of the documents to accompany an originating process.
questions.law_reference: Frontloading — statement of claim filed with writ

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. Dr. Lukeman was unconscious and was rushed to the hospital where he received treatment worth of ₦10 million. He has decided to sue Mr. Holloway and Better Sand Nigeria Ltd at the High Court of the FCT, Gwagalada Judicial Division for negligence.
questions.question: Within how many days are the defendants expected to file their statement of defence after being served with the originating processes?
questions.explanation: A defendant in Abuja is expected to file his statement of defence within 21 days after being served with the originating process. Order 15, Rule 1(2), Abuja.

questions.question: All but one of the following are functions of pleadings:
options: It serves as evidence to prove a party's case
It constitute permanent records before the Court
It shows on whom lies the burden of proof
Defines and ascertain the issues between the parties
questions.explanation: Pleadings do not constitute evidence before the court.

questions.question: In all but one of the following, amendment of pleadings will be permitted by the Court
options: For the purpose of determining the real issues raised in the proceeding
To bring pleadings in line with evidence already adduced
To include facts not in existence at the time of commencement of the action but now seems necessary
To correct a misnomer in the name of the parties
questions.explanation: Amendment of pleadings would not be allowed to accommodate facts which were not in existence at the time the action was commenced. See Gowon v Ike-Okongwu.

questions.question: Assuming the matter between the parties has proceeded to trial, what's the maximum number of times parties can seek to amend their pleadings during that stage?
options: Amendment is not allowed during trial
