questions.explanation: Order 25, Rule 1, Abuja. A party will not be allowed to amend his pleadings more than twice during trial. However, there is no limit to the number of times pleadings can be amended during a pre-trial or case management conference.

questions.question: The most appropriate procedure for amendment of pleadings where facts are likely to be disputed is:
options: By oral application made before the Court
By motion on notice supported by an affidavit and a written address
By motion Exparte supported by an affidavit and a written address
By originating application supported by an affidavit and a written address
questions.explanation: Where facts in an amendment are likely to be disputed, the amendment should be sought by motion on notice supported by an affidavit and a written address.

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. He has decided to sue Mr. Holloway and Better Sand Nigeria Ltd at the High Court of the FCT. Leave to amend was granted by the presiding judge to counsel to Dr. Lukeman but no time limit was stipulated for compliance.
questions.question: Within how many days after the order must you comply?
questions.explanation: Order 25, Rule 4, Abuja.

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. He has decided to sue at the High Court of the FCT. Leave to amend was granted by the presiding judge to counsel to Dr. Lukeman but no time limit was stipulated for compliance.
questions.question: Failure to comply within the stipulated time frame will lead to one of the following
questions.explanation: Order 25, Rule 4, Abuja.

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. He has decided to sue at the High Court of the FCT. Assuming the original date of filing the statement of claim was 12th of March, 2020 and the pleading was amended on the 2nd of May, 2020.
questions.question: The effective date of the pleading is:
questions.explanation: Amendment is retrospective, and dates back to the day the original pleading was filed. Oguma v Ibera.
questions.law_reference: Oguma v Ibera — retrospective effect of amendment

questions.question: In the statement of claim, the first paragraph usually contains what is known as:
questions.explanation: The first paragraph of a statement of claim usually contains introductory averments, also called matters of inducement.

questions.scenario: On the 20th of February, 2020, while travelling to the Gwagalada Specialist Hospital as a consultant to perform an operation on a patient, Dr. Lukeman Hassan was hit from behind by a lorry driven by one Mr. Holloway, a driver to Better Sand Nigeria Ltd. He has decided to sue at the High Court of the FCT. Paragraph 7 of the claimant's statement of claim contains this fact: 'The claimant avers that the first defendant was drinking when he lost control of the lorry and hit the claimant.'
questions.question: If the first defendant wants to deny that fact, which of the following is the most appropriate denial?
options: The defendant denies not drinking alcohol or anything while driving
The defendant denies that he was drinking alcohol or anything while driving
The defendant admits paragraph 7 only to the extent that he was drinking but denies every other allegation of facts contained in the paragraph
The defendant admits that he was drinking but also avers that the claimant was driving recklessly
questions.explanation: The proper denial in pleadings is a direct and clear denial: 'The defendant denies that he was drinking alcohol or anything while driving.'

questions.question: 'SAVE AND EXCEPT' is used for which of the following?
questions.explanation: 'Save and Except' is used in a general traverse.

questions.question: Only one of the following need not be specifically pleaded:
questions.explanation: All of the above — res ipsa loquitur, statute of limitation, and defamatory words in defamation — must all be specifically pleaded. None of the above is therefore the correct answer.

weeks.title: Pre-Trial Issues and Pre-Trial Proceedings
weeks.description: Pre-trial conference, case management conference, discovery, interrogatories and inspection of documents in Lagos and Abuja.

questions.question: A defendant who wishes to object to the jurisdiction of the court to try an action can do so through any of the following means except
options: Motion on notice for preliminary objection
questions.explanation: Demurrer has been abolished in both Lagos and Abuja Rules. Order 24, Lagos, Order 23, Abuja.
questions.law_reference: Order 24, Lagos Rules; Order 23, Abuja Rules

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja, parties have exchanged pleadings, joined issues and the case has been set for pre-trial conference. You are an extern in the office of Mr. Goodness, counsel to the claimant and your principal has told you to follow him to the conference to participate fully in the proceeding.
questions.question: Within how many days after the close of pleadings by the parties is the claimant expected to apply for issuance of pre-trial conference notice?
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 10, Abuja.

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja, parties have exchanged pleadings, joined issues and the case has been set for pre-trial conference. You are an extern in the office of Mr. Goodness, counsel to the claimant.
questions.question: If the claimant fails to apply for the pre-trial conference notice within the prescribed period, which of the following options is opened to the defendant?
options: Apply for the issuance of the pre-trial conference notice
Apply for the dismissal of the case
Apply for the case to be set for trial
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 10(3), Abuja.

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja, parties have exchanged pleadings, joined issues and the case has been set for pre-trial conference. You are an extern in the office of Mr. Goodness, counsel to the claimant.
questions.question: The pre-trial notice and pre-trial information sheet issued to your principal was in form............ of the Abuja Rules
questions.explanation: Pre-trial notice is in Form 19 and pre-trial information sheet is in Form 20 of the Abuja Rules.

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja, parties have exchanged pleadings, joined issues and the case has been set for pre-trial conference. On the date fixed for the pre-trial conference, neither the defendant nor his counsel was present for the proceeding.
questions.question: Which of the following represents the proper order the Court would have given in the circumstance?
options: Set the action for trial immediately
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 16(b), Abuja. If it were the claimant or his legal practitioner that was absent, the case would be dismissed.

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja, parties have exchanged pleadings, joined issues and the case has been set for pre-trial conference.
questions.question: You were surprised by the order given in 5 above and you asked your principal if the same order would have been given if it was the claimant and his legal practitioner that were absent at the conference. In that instance, the order the Court would give is:
options: Set the action for trial immediately
questions.explanation: If the claimant or his legal practitioner is absent at the pre-trial conference in Abuja, the case shall be dismissed. Order 27, Rule 16(b), Abuja.

questions.scenario: In a case instituted by George Gregor at the High Court of the FCT against McCarthy over a large expanse of land at the Maitama District, Abuja. Judgment was given against a defendant for failure to participate in the pre-trial conference and he wishes to set it aside.
questions.question: Within how many days after the judgment must the application to set aside be brought before the Court?
questions.explanation: The application to set aside a judgment given for failure to participate in pre-trial conference must be brought within 7 days.
questions.law_reference: Abuja Rules — setting aside pre-trial judgment

questions.scenario: You are a counsel to Mrs Beatrice Coke in an action she instituted against Soshine Brewery Ltd at the High Court of the FCT for breach of contract of supply and claiming the sum of ₦5,000,000 as money received for a consideration which had failed. She seeks to rely on the written contract of supply dated Feb. 20, 2020 and the payment receipt dated March 20, 2020. The original copies of these documents were lost when Mrs Beatrice relocated from her former house in April 2020. Presently, pleadings have been closed and you wish to comply with pre-trial proceedings before setting the matter for trial.
questions.question: The process you will draft and serve on the defendant for the discovery of the documents in this case is:
options: Witness summons requesting him to come to court with the documents
Letter requesting him to make discovery on oath
Subpoena summoning him to come to court with the documents
questions.explanation: Order 28, Rule 8(1), Abuja.

questions.scenario: You are a counsel to Mrs Beatrice Coke in an action she instituted against Soshine Brewery Ltd at the High Court of the FCT for breach of contract of supply. She seeks to rely on the written contract of supply dated Feb. 20, 2020 and the payment receipt dated March 20, 2020. The original copies of these documents were lost when Mrs Beatrice relocated from her former house in April 2020. Presently, pleadings have been closed and you wish to comply with pre-trial proceedings before setting the matter for trial.
questions.question: Within how many days after close of pleadings must the process for the discovery be served on the defendant?
questions.explanation: The process for discovery must be served within 7 days after close of pleadings.

questions.scenario: You are a counsel to Mrs Beatrice Coke in an action she instituted against Soshine Brewery Ltd at the High Court of the FCT for breach of contract of supply. She seeks to rely on the written contract of supply and the payment receipt, original copies of which were lost when Mrs Beatrice relocated. Pleadings have been closed.
questions.question: The form to be used to answer request for discoveries of the documents by the defendant shall be in____
questions.explanation: Order 28, Rule 8(3), Abuja.

questions.question: Pre-trial conference with respect to any case in Abuja shall be completed within what time period of its commencement?
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 14, Abuja.

questions.question: Pre-trial conference and case management conference is contained in Order____ of Lagos and Abuja Rules respectively
questions.explanation: Both pre-trial conference (Abuja) and case management conference (Lagos) are contained in Order 27 of their respective Rules.
questions.law_reference: Order 27, Lagos Rules; Order 27, Abuja Rules

questions.question: Interrogatories, which are in form____ shall be delivered within____days of the close of pleadings in Lagos State?
questions.explanation: Order 29, Rule 1, Lagos.

questions.question: Interrogatories shall be answered by____as provided in what form in Lagos?
questions.explanation: Order 29, Rule 4, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: Within how many days after the close of pleadings must a claimant in Lagos apply for the issuance of Case Management Conference?
questions.explanation: In Lagos, it is 14 days, while in Abuja, it is 7 days. Order 27, Rule 1, Lagos Rules.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: In Lagos, the duration of the Case Management Conference with respect to any matter shall not exceed
options: One month unless extended by the Judge
3 months unless extended by the Judge
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 3, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: During case management conference, what's the maximum number of times in which parties can amend their pleadings?
questions.explanation: Order 26, Rule 1, Lagos. There is no maximum number of amendments during case management conference.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: One of the following shall constitute the agenda for Case Management Conference as stated under the Rules
options: Adoption of witnesses statement on oath
Registration of the judgment of the Case Management judge
Control and scheduling of discovery, inspection and production of documents
Media coverage of the Case Management proceedings
questions.explanation: Order 27, Rule 2(b), Lagos.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: What are the processes that you need to file to commence the Case Management Conference?
options: Statement of defence, affidavit in support, witness statement on oath, and list of witnesses
Case Management notice and case management information sheet
Writ of summon, statement of claim and application for Case management conference
Case Management certificate and verifying affidavit
questions.explanation: The processes to commence Case Management Conference are the Case Management notice and case management information sheet.

questions.scenario: In order to test your requisite knowledge of Case Management Conference, your field supervisor invited you to attend a Case Management Conference. After the proceedings, he asked you some questions.
questions.question: Assuming the claimant and his counsel were absent on the date fixed for Case management conference, which of the following steps can properly be taken by the Court?
options: Dismissal of the action of the claimant
questions.explanation: Where the claimant and his counsel are absent at Case Management Conference in Lagos, the action shall be dismissed.

weeks.title: Trial Preparation and Evidence
weeks.description: Competence and compellability of witnesses, documentary evidence, primary and secondary evidence, public documents, standard of proof.

questions.question: Which of the following is true of the evidence of a child who has not attained the age of 14 years?
options: The child can only give an unsworn evidence
The child can give both sworn and unsworn evidence
The child can only give sworn evidence
The child is capable of giving sworn evidence but can decide not to on ground of religion.
questions.explanation: Sec. 209(1), Evidence Act (E.A), 2011.

questions.question: By the provision of the Evidence Act, before receiving their testimonies in court, caution is required to be given to_____
options: Only witnesses subpoenaed to come to court
questions.explanation: See Sec. 206. Caution is required to be given to any witness summoned to give oral evidence.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a form of primary evidence?
options: Copy of a document made by a uniform process, like printing
Photocopy of a public document presented in court for inspection
Copies of documents made using carbon paper
questions.explanation: Photocopy of any document is a secondary evidence. Sec. 87, E.A.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru, where the former claims that the latter has defamed him by making a public statement published in all the national newspapers that he is a quack doctor who is only fit to live with pigs. The defendant relies on the defence of justification alleging that the claimant never qualified to practice as a Medical Practitioner but only possesses a forged certificate.
questions.question: To sustain his claim, the standard of proof required of the claimant is:
options: Proof to the satisfaction of the Court
questions.explanation: In civil cases, the standard of proof is balance of probabilities or preponderance of evidence. Sec. 134, E.A.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru, where the former claims that the latter has defamed him by making a public statement published in all the national newspapers that he is a quack doctor who is only fit to live with pigs. The defendant relies on the defence of justification alleging that the claimant never qualified to practice as a Medical Practitioner but only possesses a forged certificate.
questions.question: Assuming Dr. Gbajabiamila claims the sum of N5 million as special damages from Senator Ahmed, to be entitled, the special damages claimed must be:
options: Proved on the preponderance of evidence
Proved to the satisfaction of the court
questions.explanation: Special damages must be strictly proved.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru, where the former claims that the latter has defamed him by making a public statement published in all the national newspapers that he is a quack doctor who is only fit to live with pigs. The defendant challenges Dr. Gbajabiamila to tender the document of his qualification as a Medical Practitioner in Nigeria. The claimant claims that the document has been destroyed by fire and seeks to tender a secondary evidence of it.
questions.question: What form of secondary evidence of the above document will be admissible?
options: Any secondary evidence of the content of the document is admissible
Only the written statement of the Provost of the College where the certificate was obtained is admissible
Only the certified copy of the document is admissible
Only the photocopy of the document is admissible
questions.explanation: Since the document in question is a public document, the only secondary evidence of a public document that is admissible is the certified true copy. Sec. 89 and 90, E.A.

questions.question: Which of the following is not a public document?
options: Certificate of title issued by the Land Registry
questions.explanation: An unregistered deed of assignment is not a public document.

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the candidate of the Youth must lead political party, Obafemi Thanni was declared winner of the election, while the candidate of Creative Nigerian Youth, Asundemade Habeeb, was declared the first runner up. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result claiming that the President elect was invalidly elected as a result of fraud perpetuated by the political party.
questions.question: To sustain his claim of fraud, the standard of proof required of Asundemade Habeeb is:
questions.explanation: Allegation of fraud is a criminal act, which must be proved beyond reasonable doubt. Sec. 135, E.A.

questions.scenario: At the trial of an election petition case, the petitioner's counsel, Mr. Oracle SAN sought to tender the CTC of polling unit results and the respondent's counsel, Mr. Highpriest SAN objected on the ground that only the original copies of the polling units' results are admissible in evidence. The objection was upheld by the Court.
questions.question: Which of the following is the best comment in law on the ruling of the court?
options: The ruling was right as only the original of a public document is admissible
The ruling was wrong because CTCs of public documents are admissible
The ruling was wrong because photocopies of public documents are admissible
The ruling was wrong because the documents in question are not even public documents
questions.explanation: CTCs of public documents are admissible. The ruling was therefore wrong.
questions.law_reference: Secondary evidence of public documents — Evidence Act 2011

questions.scenario: At the trial of an election petition case, the petitioner sought to tender the photocopy of the CTC of polling unit results.
questions.question: Which of the following is true
options: The photocopy of a CTC of a public document cannot be admissible in any circumstances
To be admissible, it has to be with the consent of the presiding Justices
To be admissible, the photocopy must be re-certified by the appropriate authority
questions.explanation: For the photocopy of a CTC of a document to be admissible, it has to be re-certified. Araka v Egbue.

questions.question: Generally, there are ____ types of documents in the Evidence Act, namely
options: Two, primary document and secondary document
Four, primary, secondary, public and private documents
Two, public document and personal document
Two, public document and private document
questions.explanation: Under the Evidence Act, there are two types of documents: public documents and private documents.

questions.question: The conditions for the admissibility of computer generated evidence are set out in what section of the Evidence Act?
questions.explanation: Section 84, Evidence Act 2011.

questions.question: Which of the following accurately represents the general rule of law?
options: Every person is a compellable witness but not every person is a competent witness
Every person is a competent witness but not every person is a compellable witness
Every person is both a competent and compellable witness
Every person is not a competent and compellable witness
questions.explanation: The general rule is that every person is both a competent and compellable witness.

questions.question: The following except one are ways of compelling witnesses to testify in a civil case
questions.explanation: Bench warrant is issued by the Judge or Magistrate to arrest an accused person who has jumped bail in a criminal case. It is not used to compel witnesses in a civil case.

questions.question: All but one of the following can be compelled to give evidence in Court
options: Speaker, House of Assembly of Oyo State
questions.explanation: The Deputy Governor of Oyo State cannot be compelled to give evidence in Court by virtue of his constitutional immunity.

questions.question: In civil litigation, the conditions for the admissibility of a document as evidence does not include:
options: The document must be a primary document
The document must be in a form admissible in law
questions.explanation: The document need not be a primary document — secondary evidence may also be admissible in certain circumstances.

questions.question: A competent witness is
options: A witness that is eligible to testify in a judicial proceeding
A witness that is obliged to give evidence in court
A witness that has special knowledge in the area he is called to testify
A witness that can be compelled by subpoena but summoned to give evidence
questions.explanation: A competent witness is one who is eligible to testify in a judicial proceeding.

questions.question: All the following except one are generally inadmissible:
questions.explanation: Unsworn evidence of a child is admissible — a child under 14 can give unsworn evidence. Hearsay, character evidence and opinion evidence are generally inadmissible.

questions.scenario: In a suit by the Ambassador of South Africa against Chief Lusaka over plot 12, Ribadu Street, Ikoyi, Lagos, Chief Lusaka has served a subpoena on the Ambassador to testify on his behalf
questions.question: One of the following is not incorrect as reflecting the position of the law
options: Both the suit and the subpoena are incompetent as the ambassador is both incompetent and is not compellable
Only the subpoena is incompetent as the Ambassador is compellable
The ambassador is both compellable and competent having waived his right
The suit being over land, the ambassador has no immunity
questions.explanation: By instituting the suit, the ambassador has waived his immunity, making him both competent and compellable.
questions.law_reference: Diplomatic immunity — waiver by institution of suit

questions.scenario: In a suit by the Ambassador of South Africa against Chief Lusaka over plot 12, Ribadu Street, Ikoyi, Lagos, Chief Lusaka has served a subpoena on the Ambassador to testify on his behalf. Assuming the subpoena above was rightly issued.
questions.question: It will be in the name of:
questions.explanation: Order 36, Rule 21, Lagos. Form 26, Lagos Rules.
questions.law_reference: Order 36, Rule 21, Lagos Rules; Form 26, Lagos Rules

weeks.title: Trial, Examination of Witnesses
weeks.description: Stages of examination of witnesses, leading questions, hostile witnesses, cross-examination, re-examination, tendering of exhibits.

questions.question: There are ____ stages of examination of witnesses:
questions.explanation: Sec. 214, E.A. There are 3 stages: examination-in-chief, cross-examination, and re-examination.

questions.question: The circumstances where leading questions may be allowed during examination in chief do not include:
options: Where the defence counsel permits it
On matters that are not disputed
questions.explanation: Sec. 221, E.A. The circumstances where leading questions may be asked during examination-in-chief include introductory matters, undisputed matters and matters, which in the opinion of the court, have been sufficiently proved. Permission of defence counsel is NOT one of the circumstances.

questions.question: A hostile witness is one
options: Who has refused to speak in court
Who has been a witness for the adverse party before in another case
Who refuses to speak the truth
Who takes bribe for his evidence
questions.explanation: See Esan v State. A hostile witness is defined in that case as one who has refused to tell the truth. This is because a witness is presumed to testify in favour of the party that brings him; when he turns out to be otherwise, the Court will discountenance his statement.

questions.question: In civil litigation, the following are true about examination in chief except:
options: It is restricted to adoption of witness statement on oath and tendering of documents
Leading questions can be used without restriction
Counsel cannot impeach the credit of the witness during examination in chief
questions.explanation: Due to the frontloading system which is applicable in many jurisdictions, examination-in-chief has been limited to adoption of witness' statement on oath and tendering of documents. However, leading questions are not allowed to be asked, as a general rule, during examination-in-chief.

questions.scenario: During a brief hearing in a civil matter before the High court of FCT Abuja, the following conversation ensued; Counsel: 'You are Hakeem Tunde of No 12 Asokoro Close, Abuja, a civil servant, am I correct?' Witness: 'Yes My Lord'. Counsel: 'What do you want to do with your written statement on oath?' Witness: 'You are my lawyer; you know the best, do whatever you think is right with it.'
questions.question: What stage of examination of witnesses is shown from the scenario?
questions.explanation: This is the examination-in-chief stage — the counsel is questioning his own witness.

questions.scenario: During a brief hearing in a civil matter before the High court of FCT Abuja, the following conversation ensued; Counsel: 'You are Hakeem Tunde of No 12 Asokoro Close, Abuja, a civil servant, am I correct?' Witness: 'Yes My Lord'. Counsel: 'What do you want to do with your written statement on oath?' Witness: 'You are my lawyer; you know the best, do whatever you think is right with it.'
questions.question: What type of question was the first question asked by the counsel in the above scenario?
questions.explanation: The first question suggests the answer — 'You are Hakeem Tunde of No 12 Asokoro Close, Abuja, a civil servant, am I correct?' — this is a leading question.

questions.scenario: During a brief hearing in a civil matter before the High court of FCT Abuja, the following conversation ensued; Counsel: 'You are Hakeem Tunde of No 12 Asokoro Close, Abuja, a civil servant, am I correct?' Witness: 'Yes My Lord'.
questions.question: The most probable reason why the opposing counsel did not raise objection to the first question is that:
options: There is no problem with the question at all
The question relates to introductory matters
He is a new wig and didn't want embarrassment
questions.explanation: The question relates to introductory matters, and leading questions are permitted on introductory matters.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 221, Evidence Act 2011 — leading questions on introductory matters

questions.scenario: During a brief hearing in a civil matter before the High court of FCT Abuja, the following conversation ensued; Counsel: 'What do you want to do with your written statement on oath?' Witness: 'You are my lawyer; you know the best, do whatever you think is right with it.'
questions.question: What was the proper response when the witness was asked what he wants to do with his written statement on oath?
options: My Lord I want to tender the statement in evidence
My Lord please accept the statement since it is on oath
My Lord I adopt the statement on Oath as my oral evidence in this case
questions.explanation: A witness must adopt his statement on oath before the Court can treat such as evidence. The adoption must be unequivocal.
questions.law_reference: Adoption of witness statement on oath

questions.scenario: During a brief hearing in a civil matter before the High court of FCT Abuja. The witness adopted his written statement on oath as his oral evidence in this case.
questions.question: If you are the opposing Counsel, what will you do when the witness adopted his written statement on oath?
options: Apply for the case to be stand down
Urge the court to declare the witness a hostile witness
Cross examine the witness based on his written statement on oath
questions.explanation: After the witness adopts his statement on oath, the opposing counsel's next step is to cross-examine the witness based on his written statement on oath.
questions.law_reference: Cross-examination after adoption of witness statement

questions.scenario: During the cross-examination of the claimant's witness, the defendant's counsel asked questions on matters not stated in the witness statement on oath. Objection was raised by the claimant's counsel in that regard but the court overruled the objection.
questions.question: Which of these statements is correct about objections?
options: Objections can be raised at anytime by either party during the course of proceedings
Objections are restricted only to examination-in-chief
Objections can only be raised during re-examination
questions.explanation: Objections can be raised at any time in the course of a proceeding.

questions.scenario: The claimant sought to tender a document through its witness. Immediately the counsel applied to tender the document as an exhibit during the trial.
questions.question: The next step is:
options: For the Registrar to mark the document as an exhibit
For the Judge to inspect the document and make a note as to its reception or rejection
For the Judge to direct that the document be shown to the opposing party for possible objections
For the Judge to direct that the document be shown to the witness for confirmation
questions.explanation: When a counsel applies to tender a document as evidence before the Court, the Judge must direct that the document should be shown to the other party for any possible objection.

questions.scenario: A document sought to be tendered was rejected by the court.
questions.question: What should the court do?
questions.explanation: Order 32, Rule 19, Abuja.

questions.question: Generally, cross examination is not necessary in all but one of the following circumstances:
options: Where evidence obtained in examination in chief is self contradictory
Where evidence obtained in examination in chief is substantial
Where evidence obtained in examination in chief is adverse to the case of the party that conducted it
Where evidence obtained in examination in chief is not inimical to the case of the other party
questions.explanation: The first rule on techniques in cross examination is 'do not cross examine'. Where the evidence obtained from examination-in-chief is substantial, cross examination is necessary.

questions.question: The following questions are not permitted during cross-examination except:
options: Question to shake witness' credit by injuring his character
questions.explanation: Sec. 223(c), E.A.

questions.question: All except one are incorrect about re-examination of witnesses
options: It is done by the other party who did not call the witness
It is necessary when no cross examination was made
New matters can easily be raised therein
Leading questions are generally not allowed
questions.explanation: Just like in examination-in-chief, leading questions are generally not allowed in re-examination.

questions.scenario: At the trial of a case, the only witness of the claimant has refused to attend court to tender a document that is vital to the case.
questions.question: The witness' attendance can be compelled by:
options: Subpoena ad testificandum et duces tecum
questions.explanation: Since the witness is coming to Court to tender a document, subpoena duces tecum is what is appropriate.

weeks.title: Closing Address and Judgment
weeks.description: Written addresses, order of filing, right of reply, types of judgment, non-suit, consent judgment, declaratory judgment.

questions.question: All but one of the following constitutes evidence before the Court when proper procedures are followed:
questions.explanation: Under no circumstance will a final address constitute evidence before the Court. Olaniyan v Adeniyi.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State, where the former claims that the latter has defamed him by making a public statement published in all the national newspaper that he is a quack doctor who is only fit to live with pigs. The defendant relies on the defence of justification and presents evidence by calling witnesses to prove that the claimant never qualified to practice as a Medical Practitioner but only possesses a forged certificate.
questions.question: Based on the Rules of court, after the close of the trial, who is to file written address first?
options: It depends on the discretion of the court
questions.explanation: In a civil case, where the defendant calls evidence, he shall file his written address first within 21 days of the close of the case. Order 33, Rule 14, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State. The defendant relies on the defence of justification and presents evidence by calling witnesses.
questions.question: The written address must be filed within how many days of the close of evidence?
questions.explanation: The written address must be filed within 21 days of the close of evidence. Order 33, Rule 14, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State.
questions.question: By the Rules of Court, the written address of the parties shall not exceed how many pages?
questions.explanation: Order 35, Rule 3(3), Lagos Rules.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State. The defendant called evidence and filed written address first.
questions.question: In this case, who will be entitled to a right of reply?
questions.explanation: The party that files the first written address is entitled to a right of reply only on point of law, and the reply shall be filed within 7 days after receipt of the other party's written address. Order 33, Rule 16, Lagos. Order 32, Rule 17, Abuja. In the scenario, Senator Kabiru, being the party that filed written address first, is entitled to a right of reply.
questions.law_reference: Order 33, Rule 16, Lagos Rules; Order 32, Rule 17, Abuja Rules

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State. The party entitled to reply must file the reply.
questions.question: Within how many days after service of the other party's written address must the reply be filed?
questions.explanation: The reply shall be filed within 7 days after receipt of the other party's written address. Order 33, Rule 16, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State.
questions.question: The reply shall not exceed:
questions.explanation: Order 35, Rule 3(3), Lagos.

questions.scenario: In the defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State, assuming the defendant did not call any witness but only tendered the forged certificate of the doctor.
questions.question: Who is to file written address first?
options: As may be directed by the court
questions.explanation: Tendering of document is deemed as calling of evidence, thus, the defendant will still be the one to file written address first. Automatic Telephone and Electric Co. Ltd v FMG of Nigeria.
questions.law_reference: Automatic Telephone and Electric Co. Ltd v FMG of Nigeria

questions.question: In any civil case, the defendant will address Court first:
options: If the claimant does not adduce evidence.
If the defendant does not adduce evidence
questions.explanation: The defendant will file written address first if he adduces evidence in the trial.
questions.law_reference: Order of written address — civil proceedings

questions.question: The time limit within which parties are allowed to argue orally their written address before the Court is:
questions.explanation: Order 35, Rule 4, Lagos; Order 33, Rule 4, Abuja.
questions.law_reference: Order 35, Rule 4, Lagos Rules; Order 33, Rule 4, Abuja Rules

questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by Dr. Gbajabiamila Olowokere against Senator Ahmed Kabiru at the High Court of Lagos State.
questions.question: The judgment of the court in question 2 above must be delivered:
options: Within 90 days after close of evidence
Within 3 months after the close of evidence
questions.explanation: The words used in the Constitution is '90 days'. Sec. 294(1), CFRN.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment, claiming reinstatement and damages. The suit was tried by Honorable Justice Shogunle and final written address was adopted by the parties on 4th March, 2020. However, having written his judgment and signed it but before he could deliver it, he died from corona virus disease.
questions.question: The following except one are characteristics of a valid judgment:
options: It need not contain the reason for the decision
Must be dated and signed by the judge who wrote it
Must be delivered in open court
questions.explanation: Every judgment must, among other requirements, contain the reason for the decision of the Court.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment, claiming reinstatement and damages. The suit was tried by Honorable Justice Shogunle and final written address was adopted by the parties on 4th March, 2020. However, having written his judgment and signed it but before he could deliver it, he died from corona virus disease.
questions.question: Which of the following is true of the judgment of Justice Shogunle?
options: It is no longer valid and the case must be tried de novo
The judgment can only be pronounced, not delivered by another judge of NIC
The judgment can be delivered by another judge of NIC
The judgment can be pronounced only by the Chief Judge of the state
questions.explanation: See AGF v ANPP. The Court of Appeal in that case held that in the interest of justice, a judgment duly written and signed by a judge of the High Court can validly be delivered by another judge of the same High Court if the Judge who wrote it is unable to deliver it.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment. After the close of the case, Honorable Justice Shogunle concludes that there is no satisfactory evidence upon which to give judgment to any of the parties, especially the claimant.
questions.question: The most appropriate order/judgment to give is:
questions.explanation: Non-suit is the order given where a claimant has not proved his case successfully and the defendant is not entitled to judgment. Order 38, Lagos.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment. After the commencement of the case, both parties resolved to settle the issue amicably.
questions.question: They will need to file and adopt before the court:
questions.explanation: Where parties resolve to settle amicably, they file Terms of Settlement before the court.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment. After the commencement of the case, both parties resolved to settle the issue amicably and filed terms of settlement.
questions.question: The type of judgment the Court will give from your answer in 15 is?
questions.explanation: Woluchem v Wokama.

questions.scenario: In January 2020, Joseph Aaron instituted an action at the National Industrial Court against Panorama Industries Ltd for unlawful termination of employment. After the commencement of the case, both parties resolved to settle the issue amicably and filed terms of settlement before the court. The court gave consent judgment.
questions.question: Which of these is not a feature of the type of judgment you identified in 15 above?
options: It can be appealed as of right
It can be appealed only with leave of the court
It is binding on the parties
questions.explanation: Consent judgment, though a final judgment, can only be appealed with the leave of the Court. Sec. 241(2)(c), CFRN.

questions.question: For the purpose of delivery of judgment, the Supreme Court is duly constituted if it consists of:
questions.explanation: Sec. 294(4), CFRN. For the purpose of delivery of judgment, the Supreme Court or the Court of Appeal shall be duly constituted if at least one member of the Court sits for that purpose.

questions.question: The type of judgment which cannot be stayed, which determines the rights of the parties without ordering anything to be done and is given on the strength of a party's case is:
questions.explanation: A declaratory judgment cannot be stayed, it determines the rights of the parties without ordering anything to be done, and it is given on the strength of a party's case.

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State declined jurisdiction in a suit filed by Social Rights Initiative (N.G.O) and two other persons against the Attorney General of the Federation and the Central Bank of Nigeria challenging the Federal Government decision to grossly devalue the Naira.
questions.question: The ruling in the above case is regarded as:
questions.explanation: The judgment of the Court on jurisdiction could be an interlocutory judgment if the court holds that it has jurisdiction, but where it holds that it does not have jurisdiction, then it is a final judgment. Tunji Gomez v Cherubim & Seraphim Society.
questions.law_reference: Tunji Gomez v Cherubim & Seraphim Society

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State declined jurisdiction in a suit filed by Social Rights Initiative (N.G.O) and two other persons against the Attorney General of the Federation and the Central Bank of Nigeria challenging the Federal Government decision to grossly devalue the Naira.
questions.question: Assuming in the above, the court held that it has jurisdiction, it would be regarded as:
questions.explanation: Where the court holds that it has jurisdiction, it is an interlocutory decision because the case continues.
questions.law_reference: Tunji Gomez v Cherubim & Seraphim Society

questions.question: One of the following statements is true about the power of courts to deliver judgment and give reasons for doing so later
options: A valid judgment must contain the reasons for the decision of the court
Every superior courts of record has the power to deliver judgment and give reasons later
Only courts of final appellate jurisdiction can deliver judgment and reserve the reasons to a future date
The reasons given by the court helps in forming the bases of appeal
questions.explanation: This is especially the case in election petition matters, where either the Court of Appeal or the Supreme Court is the final appellate court depending on the case.

weeks.title: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
weeks.description: Writ of fieri facias, garnishee proceedings, warrant of possession, writ of sequestration, stay of execution, injunction pending appeal.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) as general and special damages in favour of the claimant.
questions.question: By the provision of the law, how many days must lapse after the delivery of the judgment before the claimant can commence the process of executing it?
questions.explanation: Order 4, Rule 1(2) of the Judgment Enforcement Rules (JER).

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) as general and special damages in favour of the claimant.
questions.question: Assuming the claimant wants to enforce the judgment by seizing and selling the defendant's two Toyota Camry cars, which of the following will be appropriate?
questions.explanation: Writ of fieri facias (fifa) is the appropriate mode of enforcement of judgment where the movable properties of the judgment debtor are targeted as means of settling the judgment sum.
questions.law_reference: Writ of fieri facias — movable property

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The claimant wants to enforce the judgment by writ of fieri facias.
questions.question: The form that will be filled by the claimant in achieving his goal is?
options: FORM 56 of the Schedule to the FCT High Court Rules
FORM 3 of the first schedule to the SCPA
FORM 3 of the schedule to the FCT High Court Rules
FORM 56 of the first schedule to the SCPA
questions.explanation: FORM 3 of the first schedule to the SCPA (Sheriffs and Civil Process Act).
questions.law_reference: Sheriffs and Civil Process Act — Form 3

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The claimant could not recover enough money to satisfy the judgment sum by using writ of fieri facias.
questions.question: Which of the following further mode can he use to enforce the judgment?
options: Application for attachment and sale of immovable properties
questions.explanation: It is important to note that generally, the methods of enforcement of judgment are not mutually exclusive. Thus, in this particular case, the judgment creditor can embrace the option of garnishee proceedings or writ of execution to attach the immovable properties of the judgment debtor.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The claimant as the Judgment creditor wants to attach the immovable property of the Judgment debtor in satisfaction of the judgment sum.
questions.question: Application for leave to do must be made to:
options: The Registrar of the High Court
questions.explanation: Order 4, Rule 13, JER. Magistrates' court cannot issue writ of sequestration or writ of attachment on immovable property.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The court grants the application and issues a writ of execution.
questions.question: The lifespan of the writ is:
options: 6 months renewable for another period of 6 months twice
questions.explanation: Order 4, Rule 10, JER.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The court has issued a writ of execution on the immovable properties of the judgment debtor.
questions.question: The properties shall not be sold until after the expiration of:
questions.explanation: The immovable properties of the judgment debtor shall not be sold until after the expiration of 15 days.
questions.law_reference: Judgment Enforcement Rules — sale of immovable property

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only). The properties of the judgment debtor have been sold.
questions.question: The order of application of the proceeds of sale of the properties of the judgment debtor is:
options: Pay the expenditure incurred in respect of sale, pay the judgment sum and pay the outstanding balance to the judgment debtor
Pay the judgment sum, pay the expenditure incurred in respect of sale and pay the outstanding balance to the judgment debtor
Pay the expenditure incurred in respect of the sale, pay the judgment sum and pay the outstanding balance to the Court
Pay the judgment sum, pay the expenditure incurred in respect of the sale and pay the outstanding balance to the Court
questions.explanation: Order 7, Rule 5, JER.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) in favour of the claimant. The defendant as the judgment debtor wants to prevent the immediate execution of the judgement.
questions.question: He shall:
options: By motion on notice apply for stay of proceedings
By motion ex parte apply for injunction pending appeal
By motion on notice apply for stay of execution
By motion on notice apply for injunction pending appeal
questions.explanation: Application for stay of execution is used mostly for money or executor judgment. Application for stay of execution is by motion on notice.
questions.law_reference: Stay of execution — motion on notice

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) in favour of the claimant. The defendant wants to prevent the immediate execution of the judgement by way of stay of execution.
questions.question: The application above shall be made to:
options: The High Court of the FCT
The President of the Court of Appeal
questions.explanation: Interim applications pending appeal are to be made first to the trial court, unless there are special circumstances warranting otherwise. If the trial court refuses, then similar application should be made to the Court of Appeal within 15 days of the refusal. Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016.
questions.law_reference: Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) in favour of the claimant. The defendant made the application for stay of execution to the High Court and it was refused.
questions.question: Then he should:
options: Make another application to a different judge of the same High Court
Make a similar application to the Court of Appeal
Appeal the refusal to the Court of Appeal
Make a similar application to the President of the Court of Appeal
questions.explanation: If the trial court refuses, then similar application should be made to the Court of Appeal within 15 days of the refusal. Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016.
questions.law_reference: Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) in favour of the claimant. The defendant made the application for stay of execution to the High Court and it was refused.
questions.question: Within how many days must the action you identified in question 11 above be carried out?
questions.explanation: Similar application to the Court of Appeal must be made within 15 days of the refusal by the trial court. Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016.
questions.law_reference: Order 6 Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a suit for negligence brought by Dr. Lukeman against Chief Holloway, Justice Caleb Akanni of the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory entered judgment in the sum of ₦20,000,000 (Twenty Million Naira only) in favour of the claimant. The defendant made the application for stay of execution to the High Court which was refused. He now intends to make a similar application to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The following documents will accompany your answer in 11 above except:
options: A valid notice of appeal and Affidavit
CTC of the judgment appealed against
CTC of the Ruling of the trial court refusing to grant stay of execution
questions.explanation: All the listed documents are required: a valid notice of appeal and affidavit, CTC of the judgment appealed against, and CTC of the ruling of the trial court refusing stay of execution.
questions.law_reference: Application for stay at Court of Appeal — documents

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.question: One of the following is not an example of interim applications pending appeal:
questions.explanation: Garnishee proceedings is a method of enforcing judgment, not an interim application pending appeal.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, brought by Mr. Gbayi on behalf of the Adeniji family against Dr. Chris Ojukwu for causing the death of Mr. Adeniji by accident, judgment was given for the claimant as compensation for the loss to the tune of ₦100,000,000 (One Hundred Million Naira Only). The judgment debtor is found to have some amount of money more than the total judgment sum with Zenith Bank PLC, Wuse Branch.
questions.question: The judgment Creditor will apply to court:
options: To attach the sum credited to the judgment debtor under a third party proceeding
To attach the money as mortgagee to the judgment debtor
To attach the sum credited to the judgment debtor by garnishee proceeding
To attach the sum credited to the judgment debtor by judgment summons
questions.explanation: Sec. 83, SCPA.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 83, Sheriffs and Civil Process Act

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000. The judgment debtor has some amount of money with Zenith Bank PLC, Wuse Branch. The judgment Creditor is applying to attach the sum by garnishee proceeding.
questions.question: The application above will be commenced by
options: Originating summons for interim order compelling the bank to give cause why the sum should not be attached
Motion exparte for interim order compelling the bank to give cause why the sum should not be attached
Motion on notice for interim order compelling the bank to give cause why the sum should not be attached
Writ of summons for interim order compelling the bank to give cause why the sum should not be attached
questions.explanation: See Sec. 83(1) of the Sheriff and Civil Process Act (SCPA).
questions.law_reference: Sec. 83(1), Sheriffs and Civil Process Act

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000. The judgment debtor has some amount of money with Zenith Bank PLC, Wuse Branch. Garnishee proceedings have been commenced.
questions.question: From question 15 above, the bank as the 17___ has ____18 days to pay the sum claimed or show cause. What is blank 17?
questions.explanation: In garnishee proceedings, the bank is regarded as the garnishee. There are conflicting judicial authorities on whether the judgment debtor is a necessary party to a garnishee proceeding. The position of NLS is that the judgment debtor is not a necessary party.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000. The judgment debtor has some amount of money with Zenith Bank PLC, Wuse Branch. Garnishee proceedings have been commenced.
questions.question: From question 15 above, the bank as the garnishee has ____18 days to pay the sum claimed or show cause. What is blank 18?
questions.explanation: Order 8, Rule 5, JER.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000 against Dr. Chris Ojukwu. The defendant accepts liability but wishes to be given time to pay the money over a specific period of time.
questions.question: Then he should:
options: Apply for stay of execution by motion on notice
Apply for stay of execution by motion exparte
Apply for instalmental payment by motion on notice
Apply for instalment payment by motion exparte
questions.explanation: Order 39, Rule 4, Lagos and Abuja.
questions.law_reference: Order 39, Rule 4, Lagos and Abuja Rules

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000 against Dr. Chris Ojukwu. The money in the Zenith Bank PLC account is held by Dr. Chris Ojukwu in his official capacity as a public officer.
questions.question: Before attachment can be made on it, the consent of _____must be obtained
options: Minister of the Federal Capital Territory
questions.explanation: Sec. 84, SCPA.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 84, Sheriffs and Civil Process Act

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a fatal accident claim at the High Court of the FCT, judgment was given for the claimant to the tune of ₦100,000,000 against Dr. Chris Ojukwu. The judgment is to be enforced in Lagos State.
questions.question: The judgment creditor will apply to Registrar of the Court that gave the judgment for:
questions.explanation: To enforce a judgment in another State, the judgment creditor applies to the Registrar for a Certificate of Judgment.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.question: The method of enforcement of judgment whereby the Court directs two or more commissioners to seize the immovable properties and act like receiver/manager on the property without title being passed on them, but for the purpose of offsetting the judgment sum is:
questions.explanation: Sec. 82, SCPA.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 82, Sheriffs and Civil Process Act

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: Koko Akpos as Landlord of a property situate at No. 4, Egbeyemi, Ilupeju, Lagos, brought a suit for recovery of premises against Audu Akpororo (his tenant) in the Lagos State High Court. Judgment was given in favour of Akpos but the Court did not give a specific day within which Akpororo must deliver up possession.
questions.question: The judgment of the Court above cannot be enforced until after the expiration of:
questions.explanation: Order 4, Rule 1, JER.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: Koko Akpos as Landlord of a property situate at No. 4, Egbeyemi, Ilupeju, Lagos, brought a suit for recovery of premises against Audu Akpororo (his tenant) in the Lagos State High Court. Judgment was given in favour of Akpos.
questions.question: The best method of enforcement of the judgment is by:
questions.explanation: For Landlord-Tenant relationship, it is called a warrant of possession. For other claims on land, the means of enforcement is writ of possession. Order 11, Rule 5, JER.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.question: An application for the enforcement of a foreign judgment under the Foreign Judgement Reciprocal Enforcement Act should be made within:
options: 3 years of the delivery of the judgment
6 years of the delivery of the judgment
6 months of the delivery of the judgment
3 months of the delivery of the judgment
questions.explanation: Application to enforce a foreign judgment must be made within 6 years of the delivery of the judgment.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.question: Application to enforce the judgment of a foreign court under the Foreign Judgement Reciprocal Enforcement Act shall be commenced by?
questions.explanation: Application to enforce a foreign judgment is commenced by motion on notice.
questions.law_reference: Foreign Judgement Reciprocal Enforcement Act — procedure

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by one of the Directors of Keystone Nigeria LTD against one of the shareholders of the company, Mr. Saani Shola at the Lagos State High Court, the defendant objected to the jurisdiction of the court to try the suit and contended that only the Federal High Court has jurisdiction in the case. The court overruled the objection to which Mr. Saani appealed the decision of the Court.
questions.question: To ensure that the defamation suit in the Lagos State High Court does not continue until the determination of the appeal, the claimant should:
options: Apply to the Lagos State High Court by motion on notice for stay of proceedings
Apply to the Court of Appeal by motion on notice for stay of proceedings
Apply to the Lagos State High Court by motion exparte for stay of proceedings
Apply to the Court of Appeal by motion exparte for stay of proceedings
questions.explanation: Interim applications pending appeal should be made to the trial court first before the Court of Appeal. In the scenario, the application will be made to the Lagos State High Court.
questions.law_reference: Stay of proceedings — application to trial court first

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: In a defamation suit brought by one of the Directors of Keystone Nigeria LTD against one of the shareholders of the company, Mr. Saani Shola at the Lagos State High Court, the defendant objected to the jurisdiction of the court to try the suit and contended that only the Federal High Court has jurisdiction in the case. The court overruled the objection to which Mr. Saani appealed the decision of the Court.
questions.question: The time limit within which Mr. Saani should appeal the decision is:
questions.explanation: It is an interlocutory decision, which must be appealed within 14 days. Sec. 25(2)(a) Court of Appeal Act.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: The case between the parties was for a declaration of title and nothing more, and at the conclusion of trial, judgment was given to the claimant. The defendant wishes to maintain the status quo pending the appeal.
questions.question: The appropriate application is:
questions.explanation: Injunction pending appeal is the most appropriate interim application pending appeal for the unsuccessful party to an action for declaration of title to land.

questions.topic: Enforcement of Judgment and Interlocutory Applications Pending Appeal
questions.scenario: The case between the parties was for a declaration of title and nothing more, and at the conclusion of trial, judgment was given to the claimant. The defendant made application for injunction pending appeal at the trial court and it was refused.
questions.question: Assuming the application above was refused by the trial court, the defendant should:
options: Make similar application to the Court of Appeal within 14 days
Appeal the refusal to the Court of Appeal within 14 days
Make similar application to the Court of Appeal within 15 days
Appeal the refusal to the Court of Appeal within 15 days
questions.explanation: Similar application to the Court of Appeal must be made within 15 days of the refusal. Order 6, Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016.
questions.law_reference: Order 6, Rule 3&4, Court of Appeal Rules, 2016

weeks.title: Civil Appeals
weeks.description: Notice of appeal, time limits, leave to appeal, record of appeal, briefs of argument, respondent's notice, cross-appeal, Supreme Court appeals.

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho in favour of the defendant, Elemesho. The judgment of the High Court was delivered on the 1st of May, 2020. Feeling aggrieved, Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you as his counsel to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The appeal from the Magistrate Court must have been filed within how many days of the decision of the Magistrate Court?
questions.explanation: Appeals of the decision of the Magistrates' Court to the High Court must be made within 30 days of the decision.

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho in favour of the defendant, Elemesho. The judgment of the High Court was delivered on the 1st of May, 2020. Feeling aggrieved, Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you as his counsel to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: Which of the following is true of the appeal to the Court of Appeal in this case?
options: It must be with the leave of the Court of Appeal
It must be with the leave of the High Court
Appeal is of right and no leave is required
questions.explanation: Application for leave to appeal should always be made first to the lower Court unless the time for appeal has lapsed, in which case, application for extension of time and leave will be made to the Court of Appeal. Order 6, Rule 4, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 6, Rule 4, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho in favour of the defendant, Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi needs to obtain leave of court to appeal to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: Assuming there is need to obtain the leave of court to appeal in this case, the application for leave is to be brought by:
questions.explanation: Order 6, Rule 1, Court of Appeal Rules (CAR).
questions.law_reference: Order 6, Rule 1, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho in favour of the defendant, Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The Notice of Appeal shall be in Form ___ of the 1st Schedule to the Court of Appeal Rules?
questions.explanation: The Notice of Appeal shall be in Form 3 of the 1st Schedule to the Court of Appeal Rules.

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The Notice of Appeal shall carry the heading of _____court and filed at_____
options: Supreme Court and High court registry
High court and Court of Appeal registry
Court of Appeal and High court registry
questions.explanation: Since the appeal is to the Court of Appeal, the Notice of Appeal shall be headed at the Court of Appeal, but filed at the Registry of the High Court.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Rules - Notice of Appeal heading and filing

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The Notice of Appeal when filed will contain all but one of the following:
questions.explanation: Order, Rule 2, CAR. Issues for determination are not contained in the Notice of Appeal - they are in the brief of argument.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Rules - Notice of Appeal contents

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The record of appeal will be compiled and transmitted by the Registrar of the trial court to the appellate court within:
options: 9 months after service of the Respondent's notice
42 days after filing of the grounds of appeal
60 days after the filing of the Notice of Appeal
60 days after the judgment of the trial court
questions.explanation: Order 8, Rule 1, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 8, Rule 1, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho. Chief Eshugbayi has instructed you to appeal the decision to the Court of Appeal. The Registrar fails to compile the records of Appeal within the stipulated time frame.
questions.question: The Appellant should do so within how many days of the failure?
questions.explanation: Order 8, Rule 4, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 8, Rule 4, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.scenario: The High Court of Lagos State upheld the Judgment of the Magistrate Court in a landlord-tenancy relationship between Chief Eshugbayi and Elemesho. At the Court of Appeal, judgment was given yet again in favour of the defendant and Chief Eshugbayi wishes to appeal to the Supreme Court.
questions.question: Within what time limit must the appeal to the Supreme Court be filed?
options: The decision of the Court of Appeal in this case is final and cannot be appealed
Within 3 months of the delivery of Judgment
Within 30 days of receiving a copy of the judgment
Within 30 days of delivery of Judgment
questions.explanation: Civil appeal to the Supreme Court from the Court of Appeal must be within 3 months of the delivery of the judgment of the Court of Appeal. Sec. 27(2)(a), Supreme Court Act.

questions.question: In all but one of the following circumstances, leave of court is required to appeal
options: Questions involving the interpretation and application of the constitution
Appeals involving question of mixed law and facts
Appeals against the decision of the High Court in its appellate jurisdiction
questions.explanation: Appeal on questions involving the interpretation and application of the Constitution is of right - no leave is required.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 241, CFRN - appeals as of right

questions.scenario: In a petition for divorce brought by Mr. Ajanlekoko against his wife, Mrs Ajenlekoko on the ground that the marriage has broken down irretrievably because the respondent committed adultery which the petitioner could not bear at all. The Court gave an order nisi on the 30th of November, 2019, which became absolute on the 28th of February, 2020. Thereafter, Mrs. Ajanlekoko seeks to appeal the order on certain grounds.
questions.question: Only one of the following is correct about the proposed appeal of Mrs. Ajanlekoko:
options: Notice of Appeal must be filed before the end of May, 2020
Leave of court is required for the appeal to be competent
Leave of court is not required for the appeal to be competent
There is no right of appeal at all in this case
questions.explanation: There is no right of appeal from an order absolute for the dissolution in favour of a party who had time to appeal but failed to. Sec. 241.(2)(b), CFRN.

questions.question: Leave of the Court is not required for appeal in all but one of the following instances
options: Grounds of appeal involves questions of law alone
Decision involving custody of a child
Decision on the right to the dignity of the human person
questions.explanation: Leave of Court is required to appeal a consent judgment. Sec. 241(2)(c), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Robert Jude commenced an action before the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory against Bisola Thomas for declaration of title to land situate at No. 1, Palmgrove street, Maitama, Abuja. The suit was dismissed on November 30, 2019. Robert Jude intends to appeal and has briefed you for the purpose.
questions.question: When did the time to appeal expire?
questions.explanation: 3 months from November 30 ends Feb. 28 of the following year.

questions.scenario: Robert Jude commenced an action before the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory against Bisola Thomas for declaration of title to land situate at No. 1, Palmgrove street, Maitama, Abuja. The suit was dismissed on November 30, 2019. Robert Jude intends to appeal. He needs to ensure that the property is not sold by Bisola Thomas pending the determination of the appeal.
questions.question: You will need to file application for:
options: Stay of execution pending appeal at the High Court before the record of Appeal is transmitted to the Court of Appeal
Stay of execution pending appeal at the Court of Appeal either before or after the record of appeal is transmitted to the Court of Appeal
Injunction pending appeal at the Court of Appeal after the record of appeal is transmitted to the Court of Appeal
Injunction pending appeal at the High Court after the record of appeal has been transmitted to the Court of Appeal.
questions.explanation: The judgment is a declaratory judgment, thus stay of execution is out of it. Also, note that once the record of appeal has been transferred to the Court of Appeal; the High Court has no jurisdiction over anything in the case. All subsequent applications must be made to the Court of Appeal. Order 4, Rule 10, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 4, Rule 10, Court of Appeal Rules
