questions.scenario: Robert Jude commenced an action before the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory against Bisola Thomas for declaration of title to land situate at No. 1, Palmgrove street, Maitama, Abuja. The suit was dismissed on November 30, 2019. Assuming the judgment was against the defendant, Bisola Thomas who was abroad at the relevant time and came back 4 months after the judgment was delivered, and she still desires to appeal.
questions.question: What option is available to her?
options: File a notice of appeal at the trial court
File a motion on notice for leave to appeal at the Court of Appeal
File a motion on notice for extension of time to appeal at the Court of Appeal
File a motion ex parte for leave to appeal out of time at the trial court
questions.explanation: Application for extension of time to appeal should be made to the Court of Appeal, not the High Court. Sec. 25(4), Court of Appeal Act. Order 6, Rule 9, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 25(4), Court of Appeal Act; Order 6, Rule 9, CAR

questions.question: While a final judgment on a civil action must be appealed against within____, an interlocutory application must be appealed against within_____
questions.explanation: Sec. 25(2)(a)(b), Court of Appeal Act.

questions.scenario: Jane and Jude Nigeria Ltd was sued by Oaklane Advisory Ltd for breach of contract at the High Court of Lagos State. The defendant raised a preliminary objection challenging the jurisdiction of the court to hear the matter contending that it is the Federal High Court that has jurisdiction. The objection was upheld by the Court. Jane and Jude Nig. Ltd has decided to appeal the decision.
questions.question: The process(es) to be filed to commence the appeal is/are:
options: Motion Ex parte for leave to appeal and Notice of Appeal
Motion on Notice for leave and Notice of Appeal
Motion Ex parte for extension of time and leave to appeal and Notice of Appeal
questions.explanation: The only process the company needs to file is the Notice of Appeal. The appeal, being on the subject of jurisdiction, is on point of law, thus, no leave is required.
questions.law_reference: Appeal on point of law - no leave required

questions.scenario: Jane and Jude Nigeria Ltd was sued by Oaklane Advisory Ltd for breach of contract at the High Court of Lagos State. The defendant raised a preliminary objection challenging the jurisdiction of the court. The objection was upheld by the Court. Jane and Jude Nig. Ltd has decided to appeal the decision.
questions.question: The process(es) will be filed at:
options: The Registry of the High Court
The Registry of the Court of Appeal
The High Court or the Court of Appeal
questions.explanation: The Notice of Appeal is filed at the Registry of the trial court (High Court).
questions.law_reference: Notice of Appeal - filing at trial court registry

questions.scenario: Jane and Jude Nigeria Ltd was sued by Oaklane Advisory Ltd for breach of contract at the High Court of Lagos State. The defendant raised a preliminary objection challenging the jurisdiction of the court. The objection was upheld by the Court. Jane and Jude Nig. Ltd has decided to appeal the decision.
questions.question: Within how many days must the decision be appealed by the claimant?
questions.explanation: The decision of a court that it does not have jurisdiction is a final decision, and final decision must be appealed within 3 months of being delivered. Sec. 25(2)(b), Court of Appeal Act.

questions.question: The following are true of the Notice of Appeal and Brief of Argument to be filed except:
options: The issues for determination must arise or distilled from the grounds of appeal
The Appellant brief must be filled within 45 days from the date of filing the appeal
Not more than one issue for determination can be raised from a ground of appeal
All grounds of appeal including the omnibus ground must be supported with particulars
questions.explanation: The omnibus ground of appeal in civil case is not supported by particulars.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Rules - omnibus ground of appeal

questions.question: The Court of Appeal may make any of the following orders except:
questions.explanation: The decision of the Court of Appeal is either to uphold the appeal or dismiss the appeal. Non-suit is not known to the Court of Appeal.

questions.question: The following are contents of the Appellant's brief of argument except:
options: Legal arguments in support of issues
questions.explanation: Grounds of appeal is not part of the contents of a brief of argument.

questions.question: The phrase 'the judgement is against the weight of evidence' is only applicable:
options: As a form of general traverse in a brief of argument
As a form of general traverse in a statement of defence
As a general traverse in a notice of appeal
As a general ground of appeal in a notice of appeal
questions.explanation: 'The judgment is against the weight of evidence' is the omnibus ground of appeal - it is a general ground of appeal in a notice of appeal.

questions.question: An appeal against the order of a trial court refusing joinder of a party in a suit must be filed within
questions.explanation: Application for joinder is an interlocutory application, and any appeal against an interlocutory decision of the High Court must be filed within 14 days of the decision.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 25(2)(a), Court of Appeal Act - interlocutory appeal 14 days

questions.scenario: Musa Sadau instituted an action for malicious prosecution against Sergeant Ogba at the High Court of Kano State. Judgment was delivered on the 3rd of January, 2020 against Sergeant Ogba who now seeks to appeal the judgment 4 months after. The grounds upon which he wants to appeal are on mixed law and fact. He has briefed you to handle it for him.
questions.question: The application you will file to be able to appeal will be filed at:
options: The High Court of Kano State
Either the High Court or the Court of Appeal
questions.explanation: It will be filed at the Court of Appeal because the time within which to appeal has lapsed and he would need to seek extension of time, which only the Court of Appeal can grant. Sec. 25(4), Court of Appeal Act.

questions.scenario: Musa Sadau instituted an action for malicious prosecution against Sergeant Ogba at the High Court of Kano State. Judgment was delivered on the 3rd of January, 2020 against Sergeant Ogba who now seeks to appeal the judgment 4 months after. The grounds upon which he wants to appeal are on mixed law and fact. He has briefed you to handle it for him.
questions.question: Which of the following represents the appropriate prayer you will ask in the application you have filed above?
options: An order of extension of time to seek leave to appeal only
An order granting leave to appeal only
An order of extension of time granting leave to appeal and an order of extension of time within which to file Notice of Appeal
An order of extension of time within which to seek leave to appeal, an order granting leave to appeal and an order of extension of time within which to file Notice of Appeal
questions.explanation: The trinity prayer would be sought where there is need for leave to appeal and the time to appeal has elapsed.
questions.law_reference: Trinity prayer - extension of time, leave to appeal, extension to file Notice of Appeal

questions.scenario: Musa Sadau instituted an action for malicious prosecution against Sergeant Ogba at the High Court of Kano State. Judgment was delivered on the 3rd of January, 2020 against Sergeant Ogba. At the appeal, the Respondent (Musa Sadau) wishes to contend that the decision of the Court below should be affirmed on grounds other than those relied upon by the trial court.
questions.question: He must file:
questions.explanation: Order 9 of the Court of Appeal Rules.
questions.law_reference: Order 9, Court of Appeal Rules - respondent's notice

questions.scenario: Musa Sadau instituted an action for malicious prosecution against Sergeant Ogba at the High Court of Kano State. Judgment was delivered on the 3rd of January, 2020 against Sergeant Ogba. At the appeal, the Respondent (Musa Sadau) filed a respondent's notice.
questions.question: The document you have identified above must be served by the Respondent within how many days of receiving a copy of Notice of Appeal from the Appellant?
questions.explanation: Order 9, Rule 4, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 9, Rule 4, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.scenario: Musa Sadau instituted an action for malicious prosecution against Sergeant Ogba at the High Court of Kano State. There were two defendants in the case at the trial court and Musa Sadau wishes to challenge the judgment also at appeal, especially as judgment was not given against the second defendant.
questions.question: He should:
questions.explanation: Cross appeal is what is appropriate. This is used where a respondent is dissatisfied with the judgment and intends to attack the judgment on appeal, rather than play his traditional role of defending the judgment. But it is after the main appeal has already been filed by the appellant.

questions.question: An appeal is deemed entered in a case when:
options: Notice of Appeal has been filed at the Registry of the trial Court
Where leave to appeal out of time has been granted by the Court of Appeal
Where Registrar of the trial Court has transmitted records of Appeal to the Appellate Court
Where judgment has been given on the appeal by the Appellate Court
questions.explanation: Order 4, Rule 10, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 4, Rule 10, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.question: The time limit in which the Appellant is expected to file his brief of argument after receipt of the record of appeal is:
questions.explanation: Order 19, Rule 2, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 19, Rule 2, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.question: The Respondent is to file his own brief within how many days of service of the Appellant's brief on him?
questions.explanation: Order 19, Rule 4, CAR.
questions.law_reference: Order 19, Rule 4, Court of Appeal Rules

questions.question: If the Appellant wishes to reply to the Respondent's brief, he should file his reply brief within:
questions.explanation: The appellant's reply brief must be filed within 14 days of service of the respondent's brief.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Rules - reply brief

questions.question: Under the Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction, the Appellant is expected to file his appellant brief within how many days?
questions.explanation: Under the Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction, the Appellant must file his brief within 14 days.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction

questions.question: The Respondent is expected to file his own brief within how many days under the Practice Direction?
questions.explanation: Under the Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction, the Respondent must file his brief within 10 days.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction - respondent brief

questions.question: If the Appellant wishes to reply on point of law, he must file the reply brief within how many days under the Practice Direction?
questions.explanation: Under the Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction, reply brief on point of law must be filed within 5 days.
questions.law_reference: Court of Appeal Fast Track Practice Direction - reply brief

questions.scenario: In the suit brought by Babatunde Oyemo against your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of N400,000,000 representing his share of the profit from a joint venture. Judgment was entered against your client both at the trial Court and the Court of Appeal. You have decided to appeal to the apex court.
questions.question: The time limit to appeal the decision of the Court of Appeal is?
options: Within 30 days of delivery of Judgment
Within 3 month of delivery of Judgment
Within 1 month of delivery of Judgment
Within 90 days of delivery of Judgment
questions.explanation: Section 27(2)(a), Supreme Court Act.

questions.scenario: In the suit brought by Babatunde Oyemo against your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of N400,000,000. Judgment was entered against your client both at the trial Court and the Court of Appeal. You have decided to appeal to the apex court.
questions.question: Which of the following is no longer applicable at the Supreme Court?
questions.explanation: Respondent's notice has been abolished in the Supreme Court.
questions.law_reference: Supreme Court Rules - respondent's notice abolished

questions.scenario: In the suit brought by Babatunde Oyemo against your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of N400,000,000. Judgment was entered against your client both at the trial Court and the Court of Appeal. You have decided to appeal to the apex court.
questions.question: As the Appellant in the case, you must file your brief of argument within how many days of receipt of record of appeal from the Court of Appeal?
questions.explanation: The Appellant must file his brief of argument within 10 weeks of receipt of the record of appeal from the Court of Appeal.

questions.scenario: In the suit brought by Babatunde Oyemo against your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of N400,000,000. Judgment was entered against your client both at the trial Court and the Court of Appeal. You have decided to appeal to the apex court.
questions.question: The Respondent must file his own brief of argument within how many days of service of the Appellant's brief?
questions.explanation: The Respondent must file his brief of argument within 8 weeks of service of the Appellant's brief.

questions.scenario: In the suit brought by Babatunde Oyemo against your client, Dodo Osadunde at the FCT High Court for the liquidated sum of N400,000,000. Judgment was entered against your client both at the trial Court and the Court of Appeal. You have decided to appeal to the apex court.
questions.question: And if the Appellant wishes to reply on point of law, he must do so within how many days?
questions.explanation: The Appellant must reply on point of law within 4 weeks.
questions.law_reference: Supreme Court Rules - reply brief on point of law

weeks.title: Recovery of Premises
weeks.description: Notice to quit, owner's intention to recover possession, tenancy determination, recovery proceedings in Lagos and Abuja, warrant and writ of possession.

questions.question: The case that emphasised the need for the written authority of the landlord before a solicitor can proceed to recover premises from a tenant is?
questions.explanation: Coker v Adetayo.

questions.question: The Tenancy Law of Lagos State applies to all but one of the following places:
questions.explanation: The Tenancy Law of Lagos State does not apply to the following area: Apapa, Ikeja GRA, Ikoyi and Victoria Island. Sec. 1, Tenancy Law (TL).

questions.question: Only one of the following is true about Quit Notice for Recovery of Premises in Lagos
options: Quit Notice for a yearly tenancy must terminate on the eve of the anniversary of the tenancy
Quit Notice for a yearly tenancy need not terminate on the eve of the anniversary of the tenancy
Once a tenant is in arrears of rent for any period, he is no longer entitled to Quit Notice
Quit Notice is only needed in a yearly tenancy
questions.explanation: One key difference between Notice to Quit in Lagos State and in Abuja is that in Lagos State, it need not terminate on the last day of the tenancy; whereas, in Abuja, the notice to quit must terminate on the last day of the tenancy.
questions.law_reference: Tenancy Law, Lagos State - quit notice

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy, which commenced on 1st January, 2017. Miss Bolanle has been in default of payment of rent and Mr. Peter Azi has approached you to recover possession of the said premises. The annual rent is ₦7,000,000 (Seven Million Naira Only).
questions.question: One of the following must be served on Miss Bolanle in order to determine her tenancy
options: A 6-month notice to quit which must expire on 31st December
A 6-month notice which must expire on 1st January
A 6-month notice to expire at the end of January
A 7 days notice of owners intention to recover possession
questions.explanation: Sec 8 and 9 of the Recovery of Premises Act, applicable in Abuja. In Abuja, the notice to quit must terminate on the last day of the tenancy.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 8 and 9, Recovery of Premises Act

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy, which commenced on 1st January, 2017. Miss Bolanle has been in default of payment of rent and Mr. Peter Azi has approached you to recover possession of the said premises. The annual rent is ₦7,000,000 (Seven Million Naira Only).
questions.question: Upon service of the document you have identified above to determine Bolanle's tenancy, what other document are you required to serve on her?
options: 7 days Owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession
questions.explanation: After the quit notice has expired, you serve a 7 days Notice of Owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - notice sequence

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy, which commenced on 1st January, 2017. Miss Bolanle has been in default of payment of rent and Mr. Peter Azi has approached you to recover possession of the said premises. The annual rent is ₦7,000,000 (Seven Million Naira Only).
questions.question: Assuming it has become necessary to go to Court, in which of the following courts will you institute the action?
options: High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja
questions.explanation: The annual rental value - ₦7,000,000 - exceeds the monetary jurisdiction of the District Court, thus the High Court is the answer.
questions.law_reference: FCT courts - jurisdiction based on rental value

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy, which commenced on 1st January, 2017. Miss Bolanle has been in default of payment of rent. The annual rent is ₦7,000,000 (Seven Million Naira Only).
questions.question: If the scenario were to be in Lagos, in which court would you have instituted the action?
options: Small Claims Court of Lagos State
questions.explanation: This is because the annual rental value of the property - ₦7,000,000 - is within the monetary jurisdiction of the Magistrates' Court in Lagos State, which is ₦10,000,000.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 28, Magistrate Court Law, Lagos State

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy, which commenced on 1st January, 2017. Miss Bolanle has been in default of payment of rent. The annual rent is ₦7,000,000. The action has been instituted in the High Court of the Federal Capital Territory, Abuja.
questions.question: By which form would you commence the action?
questions.explanation: Action in the High Court will be commenced by Writ, and it is in Form G of the RPA.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - Form G

questions.scenario: Miss Bolanle is a tenant in the property belonging to Mr. Peter Azi situate at No. 22, Gwarinpa Estate, Abuja. It is a yearly tenancy. The tenancy agreement is not clear as to the nature of tenancy.
questions.question: If in the tenancy agreement between Miss Bolanle and Mr. Peter Azi, it is not clear as to the nature of tenancy, the type of tenancy can be deciphered:
options: From the amount paid as rent
From how and when rent is paid
questions.explanation: Sec. 8(3), RPA.

questions.scenario: High Priest Jakande, owner of a property situate at No. 20, Marina, Lagos Island, Lagos gave a term of years certain (10 years) to Olu Plumbers to be used for commercial purpose. The Tenancy agreement was made on 1st of June, 2010 and it was agreed that it would expire on the 31st of May, 2020. In order to validly recover possession of the property from Olu Plumbers on the 31st of May, 2020, High Priest Jakande would be required to comply with one of the following:
questions.question: High Priest Jakande would be required to comply with one of the following:
options: Serve a 6-months notice to quit to expire on 31st May, 2020
Serve a 6-months notice to quit to expire on 1st June
Serve a 7 days owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession
questions.explanation: This is because the tenancy is for a fixed period, and it expired on the 31st of May, 2020 by effluxion of time. In such instance, there is no need for quit notice, all that is needed is 7 days Notice of Owner's Intention to apply to Court to recover possession. Sec. 13(5), TL.

questions.scenario: High Priest Jakande, owner of a property situate at No. 20, Marina, Lagos Island, Lagos. Olu Plumbers is a licensee occupying the said property with the permission of High Priest Jakande, to recover possession, only one of the following is true:
questions.question: To recover possession from a licensee, only one of the following is true:
options: No notice is required to be served on Olu Plumbers in line with the rules governing licensees
A 6-months notice to quit must be served on Olu Plumbers
A 14-days notice of owner's intention to recover possession must be served on Olu Plumbers
A 7-days notice of owner's intention must be served on Olu Plumbers
questions.explanation: Sec. 14, TL.

questions.question: In all but one of the following circumstances, notice to quit need not be served to recover possession:
options: In case of a yearly tenant, where the tenant has been in arrears of rent for more than 6 months
Where tenancy is for a fixed period and it has been determined by effluxion of time
In case of a monthly tenant, who is in arrears of rent for 6 months
In case of a quarterly tenant who is in arrears of rent for at least 1 year

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall popularly known as 'Buy one and get one free' situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy commencing on 1st January, 2018 with a clause in the tenancy agreement that 'the tenant shall use the premises only for buying and selling'. Since market was not moving as expected, Chief Ugoh has converted his shop to a religious worship centre. Chief Apagu intends to eject the tenant from the premises and has come to brief you for that purpose.
questions.question: The first step you will take to recover the premises is:
options: Serve Chief Ugoh Okaka a one year's Notice to Quit
Obtain a written authority of Chief Apagu Agu
Serve Chief Ugoh Okaka seven days' Notice of Owners Intention to recover premises
Institute an action for recovery of premises
questions.explanation: A written authority of the landlord to the agent to act is the first step in recovery of possession. The solicitor (agent) usually drafts this in practice for the landlord to sign. See Coker v Adetayo.
questions.law_reference: Coker v Adetayo - written authority first step

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall popularly known as 'Buy one and get one free' situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy commencing on 1st January, 2018 with a clause in the tenancy agreement that 'the tenant shall use the premises only for buying and selling'. Since market was not moving as expected, Chief Ugoh has converted his shop to a religious worship centre. Chief Apagu intends to eject the tenant from the premises.
questions.question: What is the length of notice required to eject the tenant from the premises?

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy commencing on 1st January, 2018. Chief Ugoh has converted his shop to a religious worship centre in breach of the tenancy agreement. Chief Apagu intends to eject the tenant from the premises and has come to brief you for that purpose.
questions.question: The Notice to Quit to be issued in this case shall be in ____
questions.explanation: Note that in Abuja Form B is used when the Landlord is the one issuing the Quit Notice, while Form C is used when the agent (solicitor) is the one issuing it.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - Form C (agent's quit notice)

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy commencing on 1st January, 2018. Chief Ugoh has converted his shop to a religious worship centre. Notice is issued on June 1st, 2020.
questions.question: When will the notice expire?
questions.explanation: It will start counting from July 1 and must end on the eve of anniversary of the tenancy, which is 31st December, the last day of the tenancy.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - notice expiry date

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy. At the expiration of the notice to quit, you will be required to further issue .......... notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession
questions.question: At the expiration of the notice to quit, you will be required to further issue .......... notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession
questions.explanation: After the notice to quit has expired, a 7 days notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession must be served.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - 7 days notice of intention

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy. The notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession must be served.
questions.question: The Notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession will be in ______
questions.explanation: The Notice of owner's intention to apply to court to recover possession is in Form E.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises Act - Form E

questions.scenario: Chief Apagu Agu is the landlord of a shopping mall situate at No. 1, Ajayi Wafer's Close, Kubwa, Abuja. Chief Ugoh Okaka rented a shop in the Mall under a yearly tenancy. The Magistrate Court has given an order for possession to Chief Apagu Agu and Chief Ugoh Okaka is still in occupation. What should the landlord do in the circumstance?
questions.question: What should the landlord do in the circumstance?
options: Throw the belongings of the tenant out of the premises
questions.explanation: The landlord should apply for warrant of possession.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises - warrant of possession

questions.scenario: For a yearly tenant whose tenancy commenced on 1st October 2019 and is to be terminated in 2020, the notice to quit must be served on him latest on:
questions.question: The notice to quit must be served on him latest on:
questions.explanation: 6 clear months' notice (must include first and last days of the months) is required, which will start counting from the 1st of April and must end on the anniversary of the tenancy - 30th September, 2020. Thus, it must be issued latest 31st March.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of Premises - 6 months notice calculation

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy which commenced on the 1st of January, 2010 at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. The last rent paid by the company was in 2018. You have been briefed by Chief Dorcas Elumelu to recover the premises form the Company. In order to validly determine the tenancy, a notice to quit served on the Company on the 31st August, 2019 must ask the Company to give up possession on or before _____
questions.question: A notice to quit served on the Company on the 31st August, 2019 must ask the Company to give up possession on or before _____
questions.explanation: In Lagos State, by section 13(5) of the Tenancy Law, notice to quit can either expire on the eve of the anniversary of the tenancy or after. So, from the scenario, the 6 months notice required will be calculated from when it was given (31st August, 2019); thus it will expire on the 28th of Feb.

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy. The notice to quit must be issued.
questions.question: The Notice to Quit will be in:
questions.explanation: The Notice to Quit in Lagos State is in Form TL3.
questions.law_reference: Tenancy Law, Lagos State - Form TL3

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy. None of the principal members of the company could be found to effect service of the notice on.
questions.question: Assuming none of the principal members of the company could be found to effect service of the notice on, what step would you take?
options: Apply to court by motion exparte for substituted service
Apply to court by motion on notice for substituted service
Affix the notice on a conspicuous part of the premises
Affix the notice on a conspicuous part of the premises and provide corroborative proof of service
questions.explanation: Sec. 18(d), TL.

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. The last rent paid by the company was in 2018. You have been briefed by Chief Dorcas Elumelu to recover the premises form the Company. Assuming the company doesn't want to give up possession and you now want to proceed to court, before which Court or Tribunal will you institute the action?
questions.question: Before which Court or Tribunal will you institute the action?
questions.explanation: The annual rental value - ₦11,000,000 - exceeds the monetary jurisdiction of the Magistrates' Court.
questions.law_reference: Lagos State High Court jurisdiction - annual rental value

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. The action has been instituted in the Lagos State High Court. Assuming the company fails to enter appearance or file a defence, what would you apply for?
questions.question: Assuming the company fails to enter appearance or file a defence, what would you apply for?
options: Default judgment for default of appearance
Default judgment for default of defence
For the matter to be set down for trial
questions.explanation: In a recovery of premises action, even where the defendant fails to appear or file a defence, the claimant must set the matter down for trial - default judgment is not available.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of premises - no default judgment

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. Judgment is entered in favour of your client but the tribunal/court fails to specify the time within which the company must give up possession, you cannot enforce the judgment until the expiration of;
questions.question: If judgment is entered in favour of your client in the case but the tribunal/court fails to specify the time within which the company must give up possession, you cannot enforce the judgment until the expiration of;
questions.explanation: Where no time is specified for giving up possession, the judgment cannot be enforced until 14 days after the judgment.
questions.law_reference: Judgment Enforcement Rules - recovery of premises

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. Judgment is entered in favour of your client in the Lagos State High Court.
questions.question: The appropriate mode of enforcement of the judgment above is?
questions.explanation: For Landlord-Tenant relationship, the enforcement process is called a warrant of possession.
questions.law_reference: Recovery of premises - warrant of possession

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy at the rate of ₦11 million per annum. Judgment is entered in favour of your client in the Lagos State High Court. A warrant of possession has been issued.
questions.question: The process for the enforcement you have identified above shall be in force for ____
options: 6 months subject to renewal period of 3 months once
questions.explanation: Sec. 41, TL.

questions.scenario: Chief Dorcas Elumelu is the owner of a duplex at No. 2, Bode Thomas Street, Surulere Lagos which was let to Jumia Accessories Ltd under a yearly tenancy. Judgment is entered in favour of your client. If it were to be in Abuja, the process for enforcement shall be in force for____
questions.question: If it were to be in Abuja, the process for enforcement shall be in force for____
options: 3 months subject to renewal period of 3 months twice
6 months subject to renewal period of 3 months once
questions.explanation: Sec. 23, RPA.

questions.question: The following except one are contents of a Notice to Quit:
options: Name of the authorised agent or solicitor
The date that the tenant should quit and deliver up possession
The fact that failure to deliver up possession will warrant an action in court
questions.explanation: The 'threat' of a court action is not part of the contents of a notice to quit.

weeks.title: Election Petition
weeks.description: Election tribunals, filing periods, grounds of election petition, presidential elections, judgment timelines, appeals from election petitions.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. The election was characterised by intimidation of voters, snatching of ballot boxes and widespread electoral malpractices. He is aggrieved by the outcome of the election and has retained you to challenge the result of the election.
questions.question: Before which of the under listed Electoral tribunals would you file the petition?
options: National and States Houses of Assembly Election Tribunal
Government and National Assembly Election Tribunal
questions.explanation: Sec. 285(1), CFRN as amended.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. The election was characterised by intimidation of voters, snatching of ballot boxes and widespread electoral malpractices. He is aggrieved by the outcome of the election and has retained you to challenge the result of the election.
questions.question: The appropriate composition of the Tribunal you have identified above is:
options: The President and two other members
The Chairman and two other members
questions.explanation: The National and States Houses of Assembly Election Tribunal is composed of the Chairman and two other members. Sixth Schedule to the CFRN.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. The election was characterised by intimidation of voters, snatching of ballot boxes and widespread electoral malpractices. He is aggrieved by the outcome of the election and has retained you to challenge the result of the election.
questions.question: The period of time within which you are to file the petition is:
options: Within 14 days from the day of declaration of result
Within 7 days after the day of declaration of result
Within 21 days from the day of declaration of result
Within 21 days after the day of declaration of result
questions.explanation: Election petition must be filed not later than 21 days after the date of the declaration of the election result. Sec. 285(5), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. The election was characterised by intimidation of voters, snatching of ballot boxes and widespread electoral malpractices. He is aggrieved by the outcome of the election and has retained you to challenge the result of the election.
questions.question: The judgment of the Tribunal must be delivered within what time frame?
options: 180 days of declaration of election result
90 days of declaration of election result
questions.explanation: An election tribunal shall deliver its judgment in writing within 180 days from the date of filing the petition. Sec. 285(6), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. Assuming judgment was given against the petitioner by the Tribunal/Court and he wants to appeal, the notice of appeal must be filed within how many days of the decision?
questions.question: The notice of appeal must be filed within how many days of the decision?
questions.explanation: Notice of appeal of the decision of an election tribunal must be filed within 21 days after the decision. Sec. 143 of the Electoral Act, 2010.

questions.scenario: Indaboski Bahosa contested the recently held election to the Delta West Senatorial District under the platform of Nigerians Progression Party and lost. The election was characterised by intimidation of voters, snatching of ballot boxes and widespread electoral malpractices.
questions.question: The final court vested with jurisdiction in the matter above is
options: National and States Houses of Assembly Election Tribunal
questions.explanation: The decision of the Court of Appeal from an appeal arising from the decision of the National and States Houses Election Tribunal is final and not subject to appeal to the Supreme Court. Sec. 246(1)(b), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Assuming it was the governorship election of Delta State that Mr. Indaboski Bahosa contested, but lost and seeks to challenge the election on certain grounds, which of the following is the appropriate Court/Tribunal where you would file the petition?
questions.question: Which of the following is the appropriate Court/Tribunal where you would file the petition?
options: Governorship and Legislative Houses Election Tribunal
Governorship and National Assembly Election Tribunal
questions.explanation: Governorship election should be challenged at the Governorship election tribunals established in each State. Sec. 285(2), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Assuming it was the governorship election of Delta State that Mr. Indaboski Bahosa contested, but lost and the Governorship Election Tribunal delivered judgment against Mr. Indaboski Bahosa and he desires to appeal, which court will he Appeal to?
questions.question: Which court will he Appeal to?
options: Appellate Body of the Governorship and National Assembly Election Tribunal
Appellate Body of the Governorship Election Tribunal
questions.explanation: Appeal from the governorship election tribunal goes to the Court of Appeal. Sec. 246(1)(b), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Assuming it was the governorship election of Delta State that Mr. Indaboski Bahosa contested, but lost. The Governorship Election Tribunal delivered judgment against him and he has appealed to the Court of Appeal. Within how many days must the appeal be heard and disposed of?
questions.question: Within how many days must the appeal be heard and disposed of?
options: Within 90 days of filing notice of Appeal
Within 60 days of the delivery of judgment of the trial court/Tribunal
Within 90 days of the delivery of judgment of the trial court/Tribunal
Within 60 days of filing notice of appeal
questions.explanation: Appeal from the decision of an election tribunal shall be heard and disposed of within 60 days from the date of delivery of the judgment of the tribunal. (Take note that it is not 60 days from the day of filing notice of appeal). Sec. 285(7), CFRN.

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the candidate of the Youth must lead political party, Obafemi Thanni was declared winner of the election, while the candidate of Creative Nigerian Youth, Asundemade Habeeb, was declared the first runner up. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result claiming that the President was invalidly elected as did not meet the age requirement of the Constitution; hence, he should be declared winner instead.
questions.question: The age qualification for a candidate to the office of the President is
questions.explanation: The age qualification for the office of the President is now 35 years by the Not Too Young to Run Act. Sec. 131(b), CFRN as amended.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 131(b), CFRN as amended; Not Too Young to Run Act

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the candidate of the Youth must lead political party, Obafemi Thanni was declared winner of the election, while the candidate of Creative Nigerian Youth, Asundemade Habeeb, was declared the first runner up. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result claiming that the President was invalidly elected as did not meet the age requirement of the Constitution; hence, he should be declared winner instead.
questions.question: Which court has jurisdiction over Asundemade Habeeb's claim?
questions.explanation: The Court of Appeal has original jurisdiction to determine whether any person has been validly elected to the office of the President or the Vice-President. Sec. 239, CFRN.

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria, the candidate of the Youth must lead political party, Obafemi Thanni was declared winner of the election. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result at the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The constitution of the Court you have identified above is:
questions.explanation: The constitution of the Court of Appeal for the purpose of exercising any jurisdiction conferred on it is 'not less than 3 Justices'. Sec. 237, CFRN. We have Judges of the High Court and Justices of the Court of Appeal and Supreme Court.

questions.scenario: Following the recent presidential election that was conducted in the Federal Republic of Nigeria. Asundemade Habeeb has decided to contest the election result at the Court of Appeal.
questions.question: The decision of the Court you have identified above is:
options: Final and not subject to appeal
Appealable as of right to the Supreme Court
Appealable with leave to the Supreme Court
Appealable as of right to the Court of Appeal
questions.explanation: Sec. 233(2)(e)(ii), CFRN.

questions.question: The following are grounds for disqualification of a person from contesting in an election for the office of the President except:
questions.explanation: A candidate for presidential election must be a citizen of Nigeria by birth. All listed grounds are valid disqualifications. None of the above is therefore the correct answer.

questions.question: Which of the under listed is not a ground for election petition
options: The person whose election is in question was not qualified to contest
The petitioner was validly nominated but later substituted by his party
The election was invalid by reason of corrupt practices
The Respondent was not duly elected by the majority of lawful votes cast at the election
questions.explanation: See Sec. 138 of the Electoral Act for the grounds of election petition. The relevant fact is unlawful exclusion from the election, not internal substitution of candidate by the political party before the election.

questions.question: Chairmen of Election tribunals are appointed by
options: CJN in consultation with Heads of Court of state
President of COA in consultation with Heads of Court of state
questions.explanation: Chairmen of election tribunals are appointed by the President of the Court of Appeal in consultation with the Heads of Courts of state. Sixth Schedule to the CFRN.

questions.question: The Court vested with jurisdiction in pre-election matters is
questions.explanation: The Federal High Court is vested with jurisdiction over all pre-election matters.
questions.law_reference: Federal High Court - pre-election matters jurisdiction

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. Honorable Abiola Ajimobi instructed your principal Oladipo Olasope SAN to file an election petition on his behalf. Honorable Ajimobi contested the recent Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019 under the platform of the Action Progressive Congress against Kola Balogun of the People's Democratic Party. The results were declared on March 9, 2019.
questions.question: Your principal must have filed the petition on or before
questions.explanation: The day of the declaration of the election is excluded when counting the 21 days requirement within which to file an election petition. So, from the scenario, we will start counting from March 10.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. Honorable Abiola Ajimobi instructed your principal Oladipo Olasope SAN to file an election petition on his behalf. Honorable Ajimobi contested the recent Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019 under the platform of the Action Progressive Congress against Kola Balogun of the People's Democratic Party. The results were declared on March 9, 2019.
questions.question: The documents that would accompany the petition filed are:
options: List of witnesses, written statement on oath of witnesses, copies or list of documents to be relied on
Pre action counselling certificate, list of witnesses and written statements on oath of witnesses
Pre action protocol form 01, list of witnesses, written statement on oath of witnesses, documents to be relied on
questions.explanation: Paragraph 4(5) of the First Schedule to the Electoral Act.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. You observed that your principal did not advice Honorable Ajimobi on the option of ADR contrary to Rule 15 (3)(d) of RPC. When you asked him, he said he's right not to have because:
questions.question: Why is he right not to have advised on ADR?
options: Election petition is a non arbitrable matter
questions.explanation: Election petition is a non-arbitrable matter. It cannot be resolved by arbitration.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019. The petition has been served on the Respondent.
questions.question: One of the following best describes the period the Respondent must enter appearance to the petition:
options: Within 14 days from the date the petition was served
Within 21 days from the date the petition was served
Not less than 5 days but not more than 7 days from the date the petition was served
Within 7 days from the date the petition was served
questions.explanation: The Respondent must enter appearance not less than 5 days but not more than 7 days from the date the petition was served.
questions.law_reference: First Schedule, Electoral Act - respondent appearance

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019. The Respondent has been served the petition.
questions.question: If the Respondent wishes to reply to the petition, he must file his reply within how many days of being served the petition?
questions.explanation: Para. 4(12), First Schedule to the Electoral Act.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019. During the hearing of the petition, the petitioner wishes to amend his petition to include new prayers.
questions.question: Assuming during the hearing of the petition, the petitioner wishes to amend his petition to include new prayers, which of the following is correct
options: Amendment must be with leave of the Court/Tribunal
The leave of the Court/Tribunal must be sought by motion on notice
The amendment sought is not permitted at this stage
questions.explanation: Amendments of election petition to include new prayers are not permitted during hearing of the petition. Para. 14(2), Electoral Act.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019 against Kola Balogun of the People's Democratic Party. During the hearing, the Respondent presented multiple documents before the Court as evidence of his claim that the election was characterised by corrupt practices. At the conclusion of the trial, who should file final written address first and within how many days of the close of evidence?
questions.question: Who should file final written address first and within how many days of the close of evidence?
questions.explanation: The rule is that if the Respondent calls evidence, he shall file his final written address first within a period of 10 days after close of evidence. Para. 46(10-13), First Schedule, Electoral Act.

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019. The Respondent filed his final written address first within 10 days of close of evidence.
questions.question: The other party shall file his own final written address within how many days of being served with the first party's written address?
questions.explanation: The other party files his own final written address within 7 days of service of the first party's written address.
questions.law_reference: First Schedule, Electoral Act - written address

questions.scenario: During your externship in the law firm of Oladipo Olasope and Co. An election petition has been filed on behalf of Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for the Senatorial Election held in Oyo State in 2019 against Kola Balogun of the People's Democratic Party. The results were declared and Kola Balogun won.
questions.question: At the conclusion of this case, the Tribunal/Court can validly give any of the following judgment except one:
options: Nullifying the election of Kola Balogun and ordering conduct of another election.
Upholding the election of Kola Balogun and dismissing the petition
Nullifying the election of Kola Balogun and ordering Honorable Abiola Ajimobi be sworn in as Governor.
Upholding the election and ordering compensation to be Honorable Abiola Ajimobi for losing the election
questions.explanation: An election tribunal does not have the power to award compensation to be paid to the losing party.

questions.scenario: Chief Agbasi Obi was the Governorship candidate of Winners Party, Honorable Ajisafe was the candidate of Forward Ever Political Party whilst Mr. Mike Chukwu was the candidate of the Action Party of the just concluded general elections into Palmgrove State, which took place on 26th April, 2019. On the day of the election, the name of Honorable Ajisafe was wrongly omitted from the Ballot paper. By the result declared by the Resident Electoral Commission for the state, Mr. Mike Chukwu won, having polled 260,000 votes whilst Chief Agbasi came second having polled 180,000 votes and no votes were recorded for Honorable Ajisafe. Honorable Ajisafe is not satisfied with the outcome of the election but couldn't challenge it as he was battling cancer on the sick bed at the Mutoka Specialist Hospital. 30 days after the declaration of the result, he was discharged and now intends to file a petition.
questions.question: Which of the following is true of the proposed petition to be filed?
options: He can't file any petition challenging the election as the action is already statute barred
He must apply to the appropriate Court/Tribunal by motion on notice to seek leave to petition out of time
He must apply to the appropriate Court/Tribunal by motion on notice for leave to file petition out of time
He must apply to the appropriate Court/Tribunal by petition for leave to file petition out of time
questions.explanation: The 21 days requirement of filing election petition is strict. Once it is gone, that is the end. No extension of time will be granted. Moghalu v Ngige.

questions.scenario: Chief Agbasi Obi was the Governorship candidate of Winners Party, Honorable Ajisafe was the candidate of Forward Ever Political Party. On the day of the election, the name of Honorable Ajisafe was wrongly omitted from the Ballot paper. Mr. Mike Chukwu won, having polled 260,000 votes. Honorable Ajisafe was not satisfied with the outcome of the election.
questions.question: All things being equal, the most probable ground on which Honorable Ajisafe can validly challenge the election is:
options: That the Respondent, Mike Chukwu was at the time of the election not qualified to contest
That the petitioner was validly nominated but was unlawfully excluded from the election
The Respondent was not duly elected by the majority of lawful votes cast at the election
The petitioner was validly nominated but later substituted by his Political Party
questions.explanation: Honorable Ajisafe was validly nominated but was unlawfully excluded from the election when his name was wrongly omitted from the ballot paper.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 138, Electoral Act - unlawful exclusion ground

questions.scenario: Chief Agbasi Obi was the Governorship candidate of Winners Party, Honorable Ajisafe was the candidate of Forward Ever Political Party. On the day of the election, the name of Honorable Ajisafe was wrongly omitted from the Ballot paper. Mr. Mike Chukwu won, having polled 260,000 votes. Honorable Ajisafe was validly nominated but was unlawfully excluded from the election.
questions.question: Which of the following is not a prayer that can be asked by the petitioner in this case?
options: That the election be declared nullified and a consequential fresh election be conducted
That the petitioner be declared winner of the election having been unlawfully excluded from it
That the petitioner be declared winner for failure of the respondent to pull the highest number of lawful votes cast at the election
questions.explanation: For anyone to be declared winner of an election, the person must have participated in all the stages of the election. A person who did not participate in all these stages cannot be declared the winner of an election.
questions.law_reference: Electoral Act - declaration of winner requirements

questions.scenario: Chief Agbasi Obi was the Governorship candidate of Winners Party, Honorable Ajisafe was the candidate of Forward Ever Political Party, Mr. Mike Chukwu was the candidate of the Action Party of the just concluded general elections into Palmgrove State, which took place on 26th April, 2019. On the day of the election, the name of Honorable Ajisafe was wrongly omitted from the Ballot paper. Mr. Mike Chukwu won, having polled 260,000 votes whilst Chief Agbasi came second having polled 180,000 votes and no votes were recorded for Honorable Ajisafe. Assuming the petition was filed on the 30th April, 2019, judgment of the Court/Tribunal in the case must be delivered latest on:
questions.question: Judgment of the Court/Tribunal in the case must be delivered latest on:
questions.explanation: 180 days will start counting from 1st of May. The day the petition was filed will be excluded.

weeks.title: Matrimonial Proceedings
weeks.description: Void and voidable marriages, grounds for divorce, nullity petitions, dissolution of marriage, judicial separation and conjugal rights.

questions.scenario: Otunba J. F. Banjo and Miss Wuraola Jakande met at their orientation camp of the National Youth Service Corp, Ibadan, Oyo State and since then, had been cohabiting together in Ibadan for more than 10 years, until 2015 when Otunba J. F. Banjo decided to marry one Sisi Alawiye, which marriage was done at the Marriage Registry, Ibadan.
questions.question: Only one of the following is true of the marriage between Otunba J. F. Banjo and Sisi Alawiye
options: The marriage is invalid because of the existing cohabitation between Otunba J. F. Banjo and Miss Wuraola Jakande
The marriage is void as it was not celebrated in a licensed place of worship
The marriage is voidable at the option Miss Wuraola
questions.explanation: In MCQs, you are to choose the most suitable option. From the question, (D) is the best in the list of options. The mere fact of cohabitation with Miss Wuraola for 10 years does not prevent Otunba Banjo from marrying someone else lawfully under the Act.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - validity of marriage

questions.scenario: Assuming the marriage between Otunba J. F. Banjo and Sisi Alawiye was celebrated in a licensed place of worship and all other formalities were complied with. But two months after the wedding, Otunba J. F. Banjo, who didn't have sexual intercourse with Sisi Alawiye prior to their wedding discovered Sisi Alawiye was already 4 months pregnant, and when probed, confessed that it was for her ex-boyfriend, Fiwajomi.
questions.question: The marriage between the parties from this fact is best described as?
questions.explanation: One of the grounds for a voidable marriage under Sec. 5 of the Matrimonial Causes Act, (MCA) is the pregnancy of the wife by a person other than the husband.

questions.scenario: Otunba J. F. Banjo discovered two months after his wedding that Sisi Alawiye was already 4 months pregnant by her ex-boyfriend Fiwajomi. The marriage is voidable. Otunba J. F. Banjo intends to file a petition against Sisi Alawiye, the most probable matrimonial relief he will be seeking is:
questions.question: The most probable matrimonial relief he will be seeking is:
options: Declaration of the marriage to be illegal
questions.explanation: Where a marriage is voidable, the remedy is a petition for nullity of a voidable marriage.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - nullity of voidable marriage

questions.scenario: Otunba J. F. Banjo discovered two months after his wedding that Sisi Alawiye was already 4 months pregnant by her ex-boyfriend Fiwajomi. The marriage is voidable. Otunba J. F. Banjo intends to file a petition against Sisi Alawiye for nullity of a voidable marriage.
questions.question: The documents that ought to accompany his petition are:
options: Notice of Petition, Marriage Certificate, Answer and verifying Affidavit.
Notice of Petition, Registrar's Certificate, Answer and verifying Affidavit.
Acknowledgement Notice, Marriage Certificate, Verifying Affidavit and Notice of Petition.
Certificate relating to Reconciliation, Marriage certificate, Notice of Petition and Verifying Affidavit.
questions.explanation: The documents accompanying a petition include: Acknowledgement Notice, Marriage Certificate, Verifying Affidavit and Notice of Petition.

questions.scenario: Ishawuru got married to Eyiwunmi under Yoruba Native Law and Custom in 2010. In order to feel among and to prove his capacity as a man amongst his friends, he later married Odunyemi at the Osogbo Marriage Registry in December 2018. Since June 2019, Odunyemi has refused to have sexual relationship with Ishawuru without giving any reason. Baba Ishawuru is frustrated with everything and intends to go to Court.
questions.question: The most appropriate relief in this case is:
options: Dissolution of marriage for intolerant behaviour
questions.explanation: The marriage is void because at the time of celebration, Ishawuru was already lawfully married to Eyiwunmi, under native law and custom, which is recognised as a valid form of marriage.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - void marriage; bigamy

questions.scenario: Ishawuru got married to Eyiwunmi under Yoruba Native Law and Custom in 2010. He later married Odunyemi at the Osogbo Marriage Registry in December 2018. The marriage to Odunyemi is void. Baba Ishawuru petitions for nullity of void marriage, he must file all but one of the following documents:
questions.question: If Baba Ishawuru petitions for nullity of void marriage, he must file all but one of the following documents:
options: Motion ex-parte for leave to bring the petition
questions.explanation: No leave is needed to bring a petition for nullity of a void marriage.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - nullity of void marriage; no leave required

questions.question: None but one of the following is not a ground for petitioning for nullity of a void marriage:
questions.explanation: Incapacity to consummate a marriage is a ground for voidable marriage, not a void marriage.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - void vs voidable grounds

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa. Rose has consistently declined to have sexual intercourse with her husband until he is able to conduct a Church Marriage with her because of the vow she took in her church not to sleep with any man unless he wedded her in the Church.
questions.question: If Harcourt brings a petition, he would most likely be praying the Court for:
questions.explanation: He will be petitioning for the dissolution of marriage.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - dissolution of marriage

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa. Rose has consistently declined to have sexual intercourse with her husband until he is able to conduct a Church Marriage with her.
questions.question: The first most appropriate step Harcourt should take is:
options: Apply for leave to file a petition
File acknowledgement of notice of petition
questions.explanation: Generally, where a marriage is not up to two years, divorce proceedings cannot be instituted without the leave of the Court. But this rule does not apply in a case of willful and persistent refusal to consummate the marriage. Sec. 30(1)(2), MCA.

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa. Rose has consistently declined to have sexual intercourse with her husband. Harcourt has filed a petition for dissolution of marriage within 2 years of the marriage. For the Court to be willing to grant the relief sought by Harcourt in the step above, it will need to be satisfied that:
questions.question: For the Court to be willing to grant the relief sought by Harcourt in the step above, it will need to be satisfied that:
options: There would be exceptional hardship and depravity
questions.explanation: For leave to be granted to file a petition within two years of marriage, the Court must be satisfied that there would be exceptional hardship or exceptional depravity.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 30, Matrimonial Causes Act - exceptional hardship/depravity

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa. Assuming the petition was brought on facts of adultery, Mr. Harcourt must show:
questions.question: Assuming the petition was brought on facts of adultery, Mr. Harcourt must show:
options: That he has not himself committed adultery
That he has not condoned the adultery and finds it intolerable to live with
questions.explanation: On a petition for dissolution of marriage based on adultery, the petitioner must show that he has not condoned the adultery and finds it intolerable to live with the respondent.

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa.
questions.question: Mr. Harcourt will be required to file discretionary statement in none but one of the following situations:
options: Where he is guilty of desertion
Where he is guilty of intolerable behaviour
Where he is guilty of adultery
questions.explanation: Where the petitioner in a divorce proceeding has committed adultery; he must file a discretion statement alongside the petition.

questions.question: The standard of proof of adultery in a matrimonial proceeding is:
options: Proof on the balance of probability
Proof on the preponderance of evidence
Proof to the satisfaction of the Court
questions.explanation: The standard of proof of adultery is proof to the satisfaction of the court. Sec. 82, MCA.

questions.scenario: Harcourt Benson got married to Rose Benson on the 4th of October, 2018 at the Marriage Registry, Yenegoa. Harcourt cannot find the original of the marriage certificate issued to them at the Marriage Registry, he can prove the marriage by _____
questions.question: Harcourt cannot find the original of the marriage certificate issued to them at the Marriage Registry, he can prove the marriage by _____
options: By tendering photographs taken during the celebration of the marriage
By oral testimony of the persons present during the ceremony
By tendering a copy of the order of programme printed during the reception
By tendering a CTC of a copy of the marriage Certificate obtained from the Marriage Registrar
questions.explanation: Sec. 86, MCA.

questions.question: The sole ground for dissolution of marriage under the matrimonial causes Act is:
options: Living apart for two years and respondent does not object
That the marriage has broken down irretrievably.
questions.explanation: The sole ground for dissolution of marriage under the MCA is that the marriage has broken down irretrievably. Sec. 15(1), MCA.

questions.question: Which of the following documents will not be necessary to accompany a petition for nullity of a void marriage?
questions.explanation: Because there has never been a marriage, so there is nothing to reconcile. Certificate of reconciliation is not needed.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - petition for nullity of void marriage

questions.scenario: Lola Ude fell in love with Matthew during their resit programme at the Nigerian law school. Their love blossomed quickly and they decided to get married. The notice of marriage was published both at the marriage registry and announced in the church. The parties could not wait to be together, so 14 days after the publication of the notice of marriage, they were wedded at the City Hall by their Pastor, Rev. Canon Didi Briggs. The marriage had to be celebrated at the City Hall because of the large crowd that attended the ceremony as the church building was small. About 3 months later, it was discovered that Lola was 5 months pregnant. Matthew never had sex with Lola before their marriage as it was against his church's doctrine. The pregnancy belonged to Fineboy, Lola's ex-boyfriend.
questions.question: Which of this is true about the marriage above?
options: It is void due to the adultery by Lola
It is void because of the time between the publication of the notice of the marriage and the time of celebration of the marriage
It is void because the notice of marriage was publish at two places the same time
It is void because Lola was pregnant for another man at the time of the marriage
questions.explanation: By Sec. 8-9 of the Marriage Act, Notice of marriage is expected to last for a period of 3 months, failure of which the marriage will be void, for having not complied with the formal requirements of a valid marriage.

questions.scenario: Lola Ude fell in love with Matthew during their resit programme at the Nigerian law school. They could not wait to be together, so 14 days after the publication of the notice of marriage, they were wedded at the City Hall by their Pastor, Rev. Canon Didi Briggs.
questions.question: Which of the following documents did the parties fail to obtain from the Registrar of marriages before the celebration of the marriage?
options: Notice of caveat filed by Lola's ex-boyfriend
questions.explanation: Sec. 11 of the Marriage Act.

questions.scenario: Lola Ude fell in love with Matthew during their resit programme at the Nigerian law school. They were wedded at the City Hall. About 3 months later, it was discovered that Lola was 5 months pregnant. Matthew never had sex with Lola before their marriage. The pregnancy belonged to Fineboy, Lola's ex-boyfriend. The marriage is voidable.
questions.question: Which of these persons can petition for dissolution of the marriage as being voidable?
questions.explanation: Only Matthew can petition for nullity of a voidable marriage. Note that as opposed to a void marriage, in a voidable marriage, only the parties to the marriage can petition for a decree of nullity. The petition can only be brought by the party who is not suffering from the act that makes the marriage voidable.
questions.law_reference: Matrimonial Causes Act - who may petition for nullity of voidable marriage

questions.scenario: Lola Ude fell in love with Matthew during their resit programme at the Nigerian law school. They were wedded at the City Hall by their Pastor, Rev. Canon Didi Briggs.
questions.question: What is your opinion about the celebration of the marriage at the City Hall?
options: Appropriate since it was celebrated by a Pastor
Inappropriate as it was not celebrated by the Registrar of marriages
Inappropriate because City Hall is not a licensed place of worship
questions.explanation: Sec. 6 of the Marriage Act.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 6, Marriage Act - licensed place of worship

questions.scenario: Gbedu and Gbemi got married under the Act on the 1st of February, 2019. Unknown to Gbemi, Gbedu is a habitual 'drunkard' who prides himself in drinking only on two occasions: when it's his birthday and when it's not. This has continued for several months and each time, he comes back home drunk with little or no regard for Gbemi. Gbemi is now fed up and can't think of coping any longer. She wishes the marriage to be dissolved and has met you for that purpose.
questions.question: The first step you will take in carrying out the instruction is:
options: File a motion exparte with affidavit for leave to bring the petition
File a motion on notice for leave to bring the petition
questions.explanation: The marriage in question is within the two year rule, thus none of the exception is applicable. Sec. 30, MCA.
questions.law_reference: Sec. 30, Matrimonial Causes Act - two year rule

questions.scenario: Gbedu and Gbemi got married under the Act on the 1st of February, 2019. Unknown to Gbemi, Gbedu is a habitual 'drunkard'. Gbemi is now fed up and can't think of coping any longer. Assuming both parties in this case do not want their marriage to be dissolved, but do not wish to live together under the same roof, the most appropriate matrimonial relief to seek is:
questions.question: Assuming both parties in this case do not want their marriage to be dissolved, but do not wish to live together under the same roof, the most appropriate matrimonial relief to seek is:
questions.explanation: Judicial separation is the appropriate matrimonial relief where parties do not want their marriage dissolved but also do not wish to live together under the same roof.

weeks.title: Fundamental Rights Enforcement
weeks.description: FREP Rules 2009, jurisdiction, originating process, time limits, counter affidavit, remedies available under FREP.

questions.question: The fundamental Rights Enforcement Procedures Rules were made by:
options: The Attorney General of the Federation
questions.explanation: The Chief Justice of Nigeria made the Fundamental Rights Enforcement Procedures Rules. Sec. 46(3), CFRN.

questions.question: The Fundamental Rights Enforcement Procedures Rules 2009 is applicable to claims arising from:
options: Chapter four of the Constitution only
Chapter four of the Constitution, African Charter on Human and Peoples Rights and Universal Declaration on Human Rights
Chapter four of the Constitution and the African Charter on Human and Peoples Rights
African Charter on Human and Peoples Rights only
questions.explanation: Order 1, Rule 2, FREP, 2009.

questions.scenario: Dare Dude while traveling from Ibadan to Lagos in a commercial car was stopped by the Special Anti Robbery Squad and alleged to be a fraudster as he was operating on his PC at the relevant time. His attempt to explain that he was just replying to Emails was met with a resounding round of slaps. He was taken to custody and has been in detention for more than 10 days without any access to his family members. His sister later discovered that he was arrested and has met you to bring an application for the enforcement of his fundamental rights.
questions.question: The appropriate court with jurisdiction here is
questions.explanation: Note the decision in the case of FUTMINNA v Olutayo, which held that in fundamental rights enforcements, both the Federal High Court and the States High Courts have concurrent jurisdiction. However, the jurisdiction of the NIC is exclusive, restricted to matters arising from employment. Also, note that despite the decision in the case above, if the matter is one arising from cases where the FHC has exclusive jurisdiction, then the State High Court will not have jurisdiction.

questions.scenario: Dare Dude while traveling from Ibadan to Lagos in a commercial car was stopped by the Special Anti Robbery Squad and alleged to be a fraudster as he was operating on his PC at the relevant time. His attempt to explain that he was just replying to Emails was met with a resounding round of slaps. He was taken to custody and has been in detention for more than 10 days without any access to his family members. His sister later discovered that he was arrested and has met you to bring an application for the enforcement of his fundamental rights.
questions.question: The action can be commenced through?
questions.explanation: Fundamental right enforcement can be commenced through writ of summons, petition, originating summons or motion.

questions.scenario: Dare Dude while traveling from Ibadan to Lagos in a commercial car was stopped by the Special Anti Robbery Squad and alleged to be a fraudster as he was operating on his PC at the relevant time. His attempt to explain that he was just replying to Emails was met with a resounding round of slaps. He was taken to custody and has been in detention for more than 10 days without any access to his family members. His sister later discovered that he was arrested and has met you to bring an application for the enforcement of his fundamental rights.
questions.question: The following except one are documents to accompany the originating process
questions.explanation: The documents to accompany an originating process for fundamental rights enforcement are (1) statement of facts (2) affidavit (3) written address.

questions.scenario: Chief Jagogo brought an application to enforce his fundamental rights in June, 2020 for an alleged breach which occurred in 2018. Within what time frame after the alleged breach should Chief Jagogo have commenced the action?
questions.question: Within what time frame after the alleged breach should Chief Jagogo have commenced the action?
questions.explanation: There is no time limit to bring an action for fundamental rights enforcement. Order 3, Rule 1, FREP, 2009.

questions.scenario: Mr. Kola Ken was arrested and detained by men of the EFCC on 28th June, 2018 on alleged offence of money laundering. All efforts to secure his release by his wife proved abortive. The wife Mrs. Kola has instructed you to file the necessary processes before the appropriate court to challenge the action of the Commission.
questions.question: The best court to institute the proposed action above is?
questions.explanation: Because the cause of action leading to the arrest - money laundering - is a matter for the FHC.
questions.law_reference: Federal High Court - EFCC matters jurisdiction

questions.scenario: Mr. Kola Ken was arrested and detained by men of the EFCC on 28th June, 2018 on alleged offence of money laundering. All efforts to secure his release by his wife proved abortive. The wife Mrs. Kola has instructed you to file the necessary processes before the appropriate court to challenge the action of the Commission.
questions.question: The most appropriate mode to adopt in commencing the action is:
questions.explanation: NLS' position is that the most appropriate mode of commencing action for fundamental rights enforcement is by Originating motion - motion on notice.

questions.scenario: Mr. Kola Ken was arrested and detained by men of the EFCC on 28th June, 2018 on alleged offence of money laundering. All efforts to secure his release by his wife proved abortive. The wife Mrs. Kola has instructed you to file the necessary processes before the appropriate court to challenge the action of the Commission. The action has been commenced by originating motion.
questions.question: The documents that will accompany the processes above include the following except
options: Copies of the documents to be relied on
Statement setting out the name and address of the applicant, the reliefs sought and the grounds for the reliefs
Affidavit deposed to by the applicant himself
questions.explanation: Copies of the documents to be relied on are not listed among the documents to accompany the originating process for fundamental rights enforcement.

questions.scenario: Mr. Kola Ken was arrested and detained by men of the EFCC on 28th June, 2018 on alleged offence of money laundering. The wife Mrs. Kola has instituted the action for fundamental rights enforcement. The EFCC intends to challenge the action filed.
